text: I think that limiting it to just being work related is too restrictive. It should be whenever you have to invest in something heavily emotionally. I feel burnout whenever I have to constantly engage with people. I can work at a job for dang near forever as long as I don't have to interact with anyone, but I suffer all the symptoms of burnout when I have to constantly interact with people, and that includes my personal life.

I'm a college professor. I spent YEARS laboring under the illusion that systemic issues within higher education were my responsibility. I always told myself that if I stopped caring, if resentment overtook my compassion, I needed to gtfo. I struggled along until January of this year, when a fairly mundane but frustrating problem just fucking broke me. I sat on my bedroom floor scream-crying for over an hour like I've never done before. For the first time in my life, I wanted to un-alive myself. I knew I had to get out before I ended up in the hospital. I took emergency leave and have spent three months trying to recover. I feel like I'm just barely breaking the surface, and it's hard to remember that I was burned out and traumatized for years. Therapy has kept me afloat, but I'm still at the back of the struggle bus. However, I'm proud of myself for detaching from the environment that burned me out, and I'm grateful for my support system, who remind me all the time that I did the right thing by leaving and that I deserve a workplace that won't make me a hollow husk of a person.

Work burnout can have devestating consequences for your health, if you ignore it for too long. That happened with me. I worked a government job for 15 years, and ignored the warning signs of severe burnout. The consequences to my health caused me to have to leave my job before I was ready to do so. Don't make the same mistake. Know the warning signs, and don't be afraid to get some help.

I have had so much serious crap thrown at me in my lifetime. I reached a breaking point when I had to take a 4 month sick leave. As of late in the last two years I have lost 20 pounds, hair falling out and now I am having seizures. The anxiety of just going to work and putting up with the extremely toxic environment, I had to quit after almost 13 years. If I kept going I think I was seriously on my way out of this world. My hubby is having his 6th surgery. Been on going for the last 4 years. Very stressful. I have been off for 2 weeks and feel almost 75% better. I started to get back into my weights again, cycling and I added roller skating to my activities. I am finally having some real fun like back in the day. I also play guitar in a band. I have been focusing on more practice. Sometimes you are the only one that can pick you up, shake you off and then kick you in your butt to get going. Working out daily makes me feel 20 feet tall. I am so proud of me for self advocating and getting me out of that horrible rabbit hole I was stuck in. I have also learned to redirect negative thoughts. It takes practice though. If I can do this, so can you! ❤

I could get paid for some of that, but some down time is needed, and the rewards were some savings to be used when layoffs started.

I see a lot of people not understand his perspective. You can work a job at a Deli and be worried about Inventory or even health dept and want your place to be tip top. It applies. If you were a cashier and your stressed from the work hours you are given and 6 or 7 days would do you in. Then there is no way to "go in on Sunday". BUT what you could do is Meal prep, hit the cleaners, Clean your house, your car, do your laundry, make the day when you leave work as effortless as possible. If that's not enough and you are too stressed out from it then being a casheir is not for you.

I love the piano example as a classical pianist who struggles with this very issue haha. I have even used the idea of a creative battery to try and explain the problem to others. By making piano my actual job, I have to regularly spend some of my battery on doing accompaniment work or gigs that might not really be that rewarding for me, but I need money. But then unfortunately I won’t have as much juice left as I’d like to have to work on my solo projects or plan more exciting (but usually underpaid) concerts that focus on my passions. I’m actually trying to solve this by doing completely different work and saving more of my creative juice as a result. I’m a couple years off being able to register as a psychologist and am looking forward to (if all goes to plan) having another way to do work (that happens to be very interesting to me as well). I also just think I’m probably someone who just doesn’t like to work a lot 😂 Still figuring it all out.

I work in fast food washing dishes and making prep pans. It’s one of my best accomplishments as I get a lot of praise for my work. Problem is I feel like they’re too dependent on me. I’m the only person who will actually pull frozen breakfast product the day before, as it takes a long time to defrost. I’m also often trying to do 3 jobs by myself: washing dishes, making prep pans, and making breakfast burritos (one of our most popular items). I keep saying “I can’t wash dishes and work on prep at the same time. But I can’t work on prep if I don’t have the dishes I need”. I have someone helping me out with dishes most of the week. It’s usually only for an hour but it helps me a lot. Problem is he doesn’t come on Saturdays, which is when I need help the most. I’ve practically begged for help on weekends. I’ve had literal panic attacks at work because of overwork. Thankfully my managers are understanding but it doesn’t fix the problem

I'm post-burnout! I was a working student trying to manage a high stress career while trying to finish my engineering degree. I ended up losing everything because well... I burned out in more ways than one. Sleeping 5h per day for five years and running on caffeine is not smart but I didn't felt I had a choice. I was wrong! We do have a choice! Looking back, I should have stopped my studies for a year and then find a less demanding job and restart my studies then. Instead, I kept bottling up my lingering CPTSD and PTSD and my physical health was not doing great either. I was overweight and my body was screaming at me to stop! I eventually got really ill and got (prescribed painkillers) drug induced hepatitis as a bonus. Please, listen to your body! I'm still trying to learn how to listen to mine right now.

Probably the best breakdown of what burnout is and how it affects us. I always thought it was solely due to workload, and was confused as to why I was burned out with so few tasks to do, but this explained it. Thank you!

El enfoque que haces partiendo de House es brillante… ahora en lo que respecta a este tema la sociedad actual se ha olvidado de vivir en equilibrio, tal como en Avatar se nos enseña. No se trata de hacer todo o informarte constantemente de que pasa en todo el mundo para cumplir con las expectativas sociales de los demás, se trata de buscar tu felicidad y éxito propios para alcanzar la plenitud. Muy buen contenido, ya tienes un nuevo suscriptor. Saludos 👋🏻.

What happens when you’re burnt-out from working too hard that you begin to see your life sorta fall apart, and you lose all joy from family and hobbies. You begin to lose self esteem and start to hate that you can’t get your life together no matter how hard you try, you feel stuck, empty with no other way out. Every little annoying thing that someone does goes from zero to ten in a matter of seconds. This burnout that I’ve been going through has been making me feel depressed, like no matter what I do I can’t get out of it. It feels like I’m living the same week over and over again. Anyways all I’m say is that I feel lost, and I could really use some prayers. Thank you to whoever for taking the time to read this.

Cautionary tale: Burnout caused me to have a psychotic breakdown when I was 29. Got me the help I needed, got my diagnosis, but it didn’t have to be that way. Could have gotten help sooner, asked for it sooner, been educated on the signs and symptoms of my conditions, etc. If you’re feeling this way, take care of yourself. you don’t need to break to grow stronger; you’re not a femur.

Cause think about when you 100% believe in something and are focused. You could overwork yourself to extreme amounts and somehow still maintain the will/energy to keep going. Not to sound too new agey, but It's really important to be In a place with good energy and good people.

I experience exactly what she is talking about here, and I've always been worried that I was entering depression during these times.

This was very relatable. I am constantly overworked to the point where I hit full on apathy at work. My boss said I needed a vacation. I told him that I needed things to change at work because it wouldn't matter how many vacations I took if the culture of our workplace continued. That culture being majority of the workers didn't care or didn't show up. Those that did work, would always burn out and then leave. I hope he heard what I had to say. If things don't say I will either completely detach from work and end up leaving as soon as I can...burnout is real and the feeling is horrible.

I was laying in bed questioning myself about burnout and why I still, after 1.5 year, have so much of unwillingness to work, why some working tasks take me so much time, and that I feel just as I have PTSD because of flashbacks. Few minutes later and this lovely lady explained incredibly well. I even visit traditional Chinese medicine for helping me out, for faster recovery, but it looks like as the recovery is the same as with healing cold. It takes 7 days if you cure it with medication or a week if you let it be.

I do the bare minimum at my office job and always will. I never got credit all the decades I went above and beyond, and live by 'no good deed goes unpunished ' now. I only go in, work then leave. I'm not here for a popularity contest. I never participate in forced team building events or work outings. I never respond to texts outside working hours. I only go into the office part time. I'm too logical to be a full time slave. When I was, I was mentally unstable and always dreary. Nowadays, I work 3 days and play for 4. I finally feel balanced.

I was elated when you used the example of pursuing fame as an actor because that’s exactly what happened to me. I wanted all the things that fame could afford me and thought the only way of doing it was to be in the spotlight. I pursued my dream for ten years before I confronted what I felt all along- I was miserable and no amount of waiting for my “big break” was going to change that.I quit it all and am now working a job that I look forward to each day.

Absolutely agree. You don't tend to burn out working 100 hours per week if you're being well compensated either financially or in terms of life fulfilment. Burnout comes from being poorly compensated or being made to constantly do stupidly frustrating / inane / pointlessly menial tasks that take us away from those things that bring us joy / fulfilment / enable use to feel like we are valued / making a positive difference in the world / someone's life.

My boss had a burnout and came back after 6 months - only to find that nothing has changed and he himself also had not changed at all. He has been back for 2 months now and he seems pretty similar to the way he was in the last few weeks before he went into the burnout....So the 6 months break really changed nothing for him. Honestly I don't know why people expect that things will be different if they just go back to how and where they were. Clearly that's not the right place or the right job for you, you gotta move on, or do something completely different. Nothing is going to change in these workplaces because someone had a burnout or because a few people quit. The system can take it. Unfortunately.

I've got another thing to add to these unconventional ideas about work timings, I have a thing where sometimes on sunday night I do a bit of work before i go to bed, as it warms me up for monday morning. I found monday morning quite stressful otherwise because you're coming out of rest mode and into work mode quite quickly, this way monday starts off with me hitting the ground running and it feels way smoother

This was uploaded 7 years ago but still very relevant to this day. I've been feeling so tired lately despite having lesser workload vs a few months ago, but I couldn't get myself together and be productive like before. There would be days I'd be productive then suddenly relapse, then I'd feel guilty why I was not delivering as before. She made a lot of very good points in this video. Especially on the reasons for burnout not being directly correlated to the amount of workload but more of the leaders and systems around you. Thank you for this! Now I have a better understanding. The very slow recovery is what scared me.

4+ years of chronic insomnia, around 20 times quitting nicotine, 20+ attempts at sleep resrriction therapy, years of a crush at work playing hot and cold, 6 consecutive weeks of 63 hours at work no days off in a public facing role. Thought burnout was a myth, something id never get, just like chronic insomnia. Now saving money aggressively so I can leave my job. Hope this ends sooner rather than later

I foster neonatal kittens and I frequently run into a situation where a critical condition kitten will seen to be hanging on, maybe even improving, when suddenly they enter a period of extreme and rapid decline and pass away, seemingly out of nowhere. I can be working with them for 8 - 10 hours and they'll die in the time it took me to pee. What happened is they essentially were compensating and compensating and compensating...and finally their bodies could no longer compensate and they just hit the floor. The same thing can happen to us and our brains and bodies with burnout

I was just diagnosed with burnout. I love my job but I have the most insane and mean boss I have ever met. I can barely open my eyes because I'm sooo tired of the sleepless nights thinking about my workload. I can totally relate with what this video says about the importance of social support and good personal skils because I would totally trade my salary for one single nice word, apreciation or comment about my work. Now I'm drained and I'm on medical care and I absolutely don't know how a person like me got here! Even worse, I have no idea how I'm ever going to work again 'cause this burnout shit is really scary.

OMG, that question at 6:29 about choosing with the mind! SO me. I have a loud and strong mind - body, less so. I used to see violinists playing at weddings and think "I could do that... I could earn money that way..." Only problem I tended to ignore is that I don't enjoy being at events. It took me literally YEARS to realize my mind was totally disconnected from my real body, needs and limitations, but loved playing with possibilities for the heck of it. Honestly, I should have been writing fiction for that need but again - do you know how much hard labor goes into ACTUALLY writing fiction? Nah. I know that you adults out there have more to worry about than excessive schoolwork, and I want to be strong enough to be able to handle your situations without taking rest breaks and burning out.

The three signs she describes here are so accurate. Especially the emotional exhaustion piece where you feel like you have nothing left to give. What helped me understand burnout differently was learning that your nervous system has a capacity and when you exceed it for too long it stops being able to regulate on its own. That is why rest alone does not fix burnout. You need something that actively signals your body to come back down. I work with breathwork and cold water immersion and the combination works because the cold gives your nervous system a hard reset while controlled breathing teaches it how to self regulate again. People who come in burnt out usually feel a shift after the very first session because their body finally gets a signal it has been missing.

I would recommend talking to a therapist as well if having the above symptoms. Often times, people in this state will not admit to themselves they are struggling due to external and internal pressures, but will argue they need to be tough and a decision to do something about it, is taken late when they are forced to do it because of impaired health and possible severe long term consequences. I know a thing or two from my own experience.

i may have missed any statement on this, but i have noticed that in those suffering from burn out, they will tend to feel that even though they have accomplished a task(what ever that job task may be) they feel that it wont have worth in the "big picture". So, as an example, a student who pushes hard to get through Grad School, but begins to feel the affects of the long list of different classes they have to have high grades in, in order to achieve their diploma, may feel after a certain point in the progress that its not worth the hassle. Another example is workers. I know from talking to multiple people, both in my line of work and friends/mutual acquaintances, that if they are put to a repeated task, and though complete it every day, have it return to be done the next day(and so-forth) they tend to lose the motivation to do the task. almost in a form of Sisyphus. Repeating the same task, to accomplish it, and to come in and do it AGAIN and AGAIN. It could have some correlation to endorphins. The initial feeling from finishing the task and having the "i did it!" rush, wears over time. Soon the same task that gave you the Rush of "i did it!" becomes a "meh" due to it being just the same tasks, nothing new to it. Just a thought...

For mental burnout, take a holiday too to break the cycle! You got this !! :)

Fighting for something you believe in isn't easy. And though it's easy to lose yourself along the way, and start focusing on all the people who don't want things to change--for whatever broken, messed-up reasons of their own--you can easily find your way back. By listening to the people giving you a hand up. To the people who have your back. To the people who don't think you're a raving lunatic. Let them be your mirror--not the haters. Graduating from all the things long overdue is probably the most tormenting and painful way of letting go every pent-up hurt you've been burying for years. Hindi madaling umalis sa pag-ibig na matagal mong nakasanayan. That's why familiarity is the most unforgiving and cruelest enemy you will face and fight. Those familiar things about them will make you question, "bakit ko pinatagal? bakit ko pa pinapatagal? bakit ko pa lalong tinutulak ang sarili kong patagalin pa 'to?". Minsan, ang sagot ng karamihan, "e kasi nakasanayan". Pero hindi ba't kapag sobrang ka nang nasanay, may mali na rin. Hanggang kailan mo ba kayang tiisin yung sakit? Hanggang sa maubos ka? Hanggang sa paulit-ulit ka nang hampasin at gisingin ng mga kaibigan mo para mahimasmasan at magising na sa katotohanan. Pampahulas kumbaga. The familiarity of being in love with that person will definitely make, and at some point maybe break, your youth. But since we believe in the saying that experience is the best teacher, todo sugal tayo. Kaya tayo nahihirapan, kasi choice nating magstay, and to you who thinks that maybe it's wrong to choose this path, that your choice to stay is wrong, isang mahigpit na yakap para sa'yo, para sa'tin. Hindi madaling umalis sa pag-ibig na nakasanayan. Sa pag-ibig na akala mo'y may patutunguhan. Sa pag-ibig na akala mo'y may wakas. At lalo't higit sa pag-ibig na akala mo'y pwede. Pero one thing's for sure, kagaya ni Carson, makakagraduate din tayo. Makakaalis din tayo sa akala nating panghabang-buhay nating makakasanayan. At kapag dumating na ang oras na 'yon, ipagtatagay kita, happy graduation beh, deserve mong sumaya.

My favorite combo to stave off burnout, but you need some privilege to be able to take about 3 days off I'm a row: Day 1, do almost nothing, chill out, don't look at social media or the news much either. Just sleep in and be with your thoughts, maybe light chores. Day 2: full body deep tissue massage, then go to a sensory deprivation salt float tank, then go to an infrared sauna pod. Holy Trinity, especially as the weather is starting to cool down in the Fall. 😅 Day 3: do whatever you want but don't over do it. Probably a good day to reconnect with loved ones so you don't feel too lonely. Do some light socializing in person if possible.

Burnout was what lead me to ADHD treatment... except it took almost 4 years because no one once mentioned ADHD as a possibility to me. I was "depressed and anxious". Channels like yours made me realise what the problem was and after starting treatment less than 2 months ago I'm finally returning to work successfully. They made me realise that I wasn't lazy or incapable when I shut down after taking on too much. I'm almost 34. It's hard not to lament the potential that I've already lost but instead, I'm trying to look to the future. To get my finances and physical health back on track (hi ADHD) to repair relationships I let fall to the wayside (hi ADHD) to be less scared of not fitting in with my peers (hi ADHD) to stop making big plans that I can't follow through with and focus on the manageable and desirable (hi ADHD). So thank you Jess.

Here in Sweden we have special clinics for burnout, and the criteria is different than these three you describe here. It has to do with changes in the brain (this has been proven) where you experience memory loss, you can't concentrate at all, information just doesn't stick, you can forget where you live or where you are going sitting in the car driving the same road you do every day etc. It's scary stuff. The body simply "stops functioning" and you can't get out of bed or you pass out due to exhaustion. It's so much more serious than "you don't feel like going to work". Recovery, if you have a serious burnout, can take years. It doesn't need to include depression, but some people have that at the same time. This is a serious syndrome, if you really have burnout syndrome it wont matter how much you WANT to get back to work and life, it wont help with positive thinking or just a little rest. It's a serious, serious syndrome that needs to be taken very seriously. But it's positive that this gets more recognition in other countries as well. Many get diagnosed with depression, but that is a different thing.

Here in 2021...it strikes me how much worse this can be during the pandemic. On the one hand, you’re grateful to have a job and flexibility be working from home, and on the other you’re absorbing more work than ever, there’a no distancing yourself home life from your work life, many are caring for children or family members while working, and there’s no vacation or social time to wind down. Add in all the other stressed and trauma from the pandemic losses and it’s a recipe for serious burnout. It also comes with how companies treat workers, how they recruit talent, etc. Even the process of applying for new jobs is taxing with multiple rounds of interviews, the stress of having to take time off just to get a new job, the inability to quit a job when it’s toxic or when you know you need to move on, because you need every paycheck and you rely on your employer for healthcare. Just to name a few. The pandemic has pointed to so many ways our systems in the US are broken, our labor system is but one of them. We need to change our ways. Imagine living in a world where people are treated like human beings instead of human capital, where we are treated like stakeholders, when we are permitted to take breaks, or have wants and needs that we are equipped to respond to. This burnout game is a lose/lose situation for absolutely everyone, and it all comes from systems of power.

Also important to mention.. its not just your job that causes burnout its a combo of everything in your life from past trauma, mental health conditions, an abusive marriage, toxic friends or family which can vary from person to person.. everything in your life affects your mind and its about time we stop forcing people to 'separate work and personal life'.. youre one person with the same heart and brain that goes to work and comes home.. everything in your life has a concious or subconcious influence on you.. no matter how much you suppress, your body remembers hence the burn out..

I didn’t realize I was burned out until I started seeing all these signs in myself. Feeling constantly exhausted, losing interest in things I used to enjoy, and being easily irritated had become my “normal.” I blamed myself for lacking discipline or motivation, but it wasn’t that at all. My brain was overstimulated, my sleep was off, and I was trapped in a loop that drained me every day. What finally helped me was discovering a structured 30-day reset that focused on rebuilding energy, improving sleep, and restoring focus step by step. Within days, I noticed small but meaningful changes. Mornings felt lighter, I could concentrate better, and hobbies became enjoyable again. It gave me back a sense of control I didn’t realize I had lost. If you’re noticing these signs in yourself, don’t ignore them. Taking intentional steps to reset can change everything. The guide that helped me is Reset Your Body and Mind by Morgan Lane, and it honestly felt like a lifeline when I needed it most.

I'm in my 30s, and I really wish I knew that giving information time to ruminate will result in further ideas and superior retention of information when I graduated high school. I think this method would also help with creative burnout. As someone who was stuck in a soul-sucking job in medical billing for four years, all of my creative energy was drained out of me. Throw in some other factors and I have experienced a complete creativity / motivation burnout. I'm finally working on creative hobbies again. It's been such a fight.

I always thought my exhaustion and lack of focus meant I was lazy or not disciplined enough. Watching this video hit me hard performance debt makes so much sense. I’ve been pushing myself nonstop, juggling work, scrolling endlessly on my phone, and trying to keep up with everything, but my brain was just shutting down. What finally helped me was discovering a 30-day reset focused on rebuilding energy, sleep, and focus in realistic steps. Slowly, I started noticing mornings felt lighter, my attention sharpened, and I could enjoy small things again. It wasn’t about working harder it was about recovering smarter and giving myself permission to reset. If you feel like your brain is constantly drained, even small intentional steps can make a huge difference. The guide that helped me is Reset Your Body and Mind by Morgan Lane, and it honestly helped me regain control over my energy and focus.

Burnout recovery is so much more than just taking a vacation. What helped me the most was learning to reset my nervous system daily instead of waiting until I was completely empty. Even five minutes of intentional breathwork in the morning made a noticeable difference in how I handled stress throughout the day. The body holds tension from burnout in ways we do not consciously notice until we actively release it. I started facilitating breathwork sessions specifically because I watched so many people including myself recover from chronic stress by learning how to regulate their own nervous system through the breath.

I think this is why I, as an austistic person , struggle so much with doing mindless meaningless tasks in the workplace. Because it comes natural to me to analyze everything and try to make sense of what I am doing on the larger scope of the company/society. It's even harder when I find it makes no sense because my brain automatically shuts down the motivation to do it. This generally makes me end in an unmotivated loop of self-doubt about my abilities and purpose.

My last job caused me both. I worked for a corporate coffee company that was majorly always understaffed and had unrealistic expectations at all times. The drive thru was insane with line times when you have no staff (which was always) and staff who wanted to follow health dept cleaning standards never could because the line times were prioritied above wverything else. But the customers never thought oh.... they are always understaffed.... maybe they arent being able to clean as well... I dont eat at any of the locations now... 😅 I am still recovering 3 months later. I sleep regularly now, make sure im eating healthy and mentally spending a lot of time talking to my Higher Power and having down time. I've been getting back into hobbies, being in nature and doing school now to get to a meaningful job. Its slowly getting better

I don't care how damn balanced you were before that, YOU WILL BE BURNED OUT after this! To think that trauma is the only cause of burnout is complete idiocy.

As someone that has gone through several burnouts, due to personal and work circumstances, I think it should be mentioned that no only high achievers can experience burnout. At some point I was targeted by management to be harassed out of the company, as a merger meant they didn't need me, but they had signed a contract to not fire anyone. Instead of working with me and being open and honest, they decided to torture me, to make me leave on my own account......which didn't work. Long story, but I won a ton of money, but I was emotionally broken. Simple tasks would take extreme effort, and concentration was no option. Years later I was in a relationship with someone that had several mental issues, and tried to commit suicide almost on a daily basis. As you can imagine, this brought on a lot of stress. The biggest indicator for me was the inability to focus/concentrate on work, even when I was at the office and I knew my partner was safe. I couldn't finish a simple email and got lost in simple tasks. Also keep in mind that some of the mentioned symptoms are not exclusive to burnout. Emotion numbness is also common in depression and other situations.

As a fellow academic, i find it really very insulting that she said "oh i just took a sabbatical to fix my issues". Most people can't do that, and suggestions like that are utterly pointless. The burnout stems *from* an inability to stop working, an inability to control your circumstances, little to no reward for your work, and the apathy that comes with those things. This speaker is a professor, head of department (i.e. has been heavily rewarded for their work), and was able to take a sabbatical (i.e. has complete control of their workflow) and they have the audacity to pretend that this is any sort of solution for the vast majority of people? Further, while she does - very briefly - talk about organisations needing to change their approach to work, she spends most of her time talking about how *you* need to change *your* approach to work. Cause clearly, its the worker that's mostly at fault here. *You* haven't managed *your* workload effectively, *you* haven't taken time for *your* hobbies and interests, *you* identify too strongly with *your* work. Shifting blame and responsibility onto the worker and not the employer or the structural systems behind the work... Horrible attitude. Even worse that its held by a researcher in the field.

As a stay at home mom, I live in my workplace 24/7 and it’s so easy to get caught up in efficiency because there is so much and so many people to take care of. Especially when someone is always home so alone time only exists by being in my bedroom so I’ve lost whatever carefree silliness I had left after growing up in a high demand religion that taught me that I am only saved through my works, that taught me that grace alone isn’t sufficient to be saved and be with my family for eternity. Women take on the burden of keeping the house running efficiently and staying clean. Not know what was an appropriate workload to add to my kids who were full time students and a spouse who is at a job full time (in a way that is measurable because I realize that stay at home work is more than full time it’s just so sporadic throughly the day that it’s hard to measure so it’s hard to see the real magnitude of the workload) somehow became me doing all the work, not because they were unwilling but because I couldn’t figure out what was fair to ask for. That feeling of “if I don’t do it nobody else will” then became the consequence of that. Then it becomes hard to hear, it’s okay if the floor isn’t clean this week because I secretly know it’s not just a one week lapse but it’s been 4 weeks and if I add another week it feels unacceptable Seeing my friends have very clean houses adds to the pressure. Knowing that I’m the only naturally tidy person also adds to everything. I have seen a therapist about this and it became a topic in couple’s therapy as well. I have to say that therapy helps a lot. I still struggle with it and depending on what’s going on in the family, we backslide into me doing too much, but if anyone is wondering if therapy can help, it absolutely can. I had to start small with finding my identity because it was lost long before I became a wife and mom and stay at home partner. It’s a work in progress. I want to be fun and silly, I see that in others and wish I could let myself be that way, that’s what my next work with my therapist is all about. I didn’t know “having fun” was a valid core value and it has been hard for me to accept. I started taking voice lessons and joined a karaoke group as part of this journey, and that work and goal oriented expectation even showed up there. I told someone in my karaoke group about my lessons when he noticed my improvements and his first question was asking me what I plan to do with those lessons, what my end goal was. When I said I’d like to maybe perform someday but if I never do it’s all about the fact that dining is fun for me, he looked quite surprised that if all it ever was was for enjoyment that was enough.

This content, these videos... have saved me so many times over the last few years. To make sense of these scary feelings and thoughts that initially I did not understand but most importantly - I thought I was ALONE in these feelings/thoughts. The only person who felt this way. The times I thought I was really going crazy and that my life as I knew it was over. I don't know what I would have done without this type of content and information available to us. Yes therapy is a great help and so are my meds- But most importantly- knowing we are not alone in these feelings- and being kinder to ourselves is really important. Reading these comments whilst they make me so sad so much suffering is going on and the hardships so many strangers out there have/are going through, I realise I am NOT ALONE and your comments give me strength too. Sincerely- so much love to you all. ❤

Vì Mẹ em cũng đã nói chồng em là Bê Đê như thế. Nói chồng em bệnh hoạn lây cho em và con em.... Em từng li hôn vì 1 người chồng vô tâm, em nuôi con 1 mình và rất sợ lời mật ngọt của đàn ông. Rồi em gặp chồng em hiện tại. Anh ấy là tomboy hay nói đúng hơn là transguy. Anh ấy thương yêu 2 mẹ con em, quan tâm và cưng chiều 2 mẹ con em hết mực. Em và anh ấy sống cùng nhau được hơn 4 năm. Em đang phụng dưỡng mẹ ruột của em. 4 năm qua anh về ở với em, lo lắng cho em, con và thương luôn cả mẹ. Nhưng đời không như là mơ. Suốt 4 năm mấy qua anh vẫn không lấy được tình thương của mẹ. Trong mắt mẹ anh mãi là thằng Bê Đê.... Khoảng 2 tuần trước, Mẹ bảo em rằng, mày bớt cho con mày tiếp xúc với nó lại, kẻo lây cái bệnh hoạn... Trong khi anh đã chăm lo cho con từ 4 tuổi đến nay đã gần 9 tuổi rồi. Con rất mến và thương anh hơn cả em. Những lời nói cay nghiệt của mẹ làm ngày hôm đó anh muốn chết đi sống lại. Ngày hôm ấy anh muốn giết cả gđ nhỏ này rồi anh tự giận. Khóc hết cả nước mắt, mấy ngày anh mới lấy lại tinh thần. Em khuyên anh thôi bỏ đi rồi qua năm sau mình lấy số tiền dành dụm đi xứ khác ở. Chứ hiện tại em đã không thể nào ở cùng với mẹ được nữa. Thôi thì phụng dưỡng mẹ sẽ còn có các anh và chị. Con là gái út coi như theo chồng không làm tròn lời hứa. Chứ cái hoàn cảnh hiện tại thì cũng có ngày sẽ đứt dây... Em vì hạnh phúc của em tiếp tục đi lập nghiệp xứ người. Cuộc sống lại 1 lần nữa sẽ thay đổi nhưng em nghĩ sẽ tích cực hơn hiện tại.... Thực sự thấy những bạn LGBT em rất thương và không hề phân biệt đối xử. Vì ánh mắt người đời đã quá cay độc với họ rồi nên mình hãy dịu dàng với họ. Giờ em buồn lắm. I started listening to this podcast just to learn about cortisol because last night I heard someone talking about it and I wanted to understand what the discussion was about. Wow! Mind blowing how the Universe actually set this out to learn what I am doing wrong! All my adult life I was more energetic in the morning and exhausted in the evening, which was ok apart from me attending social events. But for the past year I started taking Ashwagandha, magnesium and theanine first thing in the morning along with my ADHD medication. My morning energy has disappeared and I only have a bit of energy in the afternoon, but not enough to at least do my chores. From now on I will take these supplements after my dinner, I knew I was doing something wrong but didn't know what. Thanks a lot for all the time and effort into educating people, I am so grateful for bringing clarity and for making us realise that we need to make our own efforts to keep healthy or to heal ourselves. Love from Romania ❤

I actually completely disagree with her, although I understand where she is coming from. I worked a job for 7 years where I felt I was making a true difference in people's lives. I loved my job. I loved my coworkers. But the management was toxic. And it was simply BECAUSE I loved my job that I became burnt out. Like the Papa Roach song, my weakness is that I care too much. I had way too many emotions invested in my job, where I made $34,000 a year. A job is a job. We are not defined by our jobs. I don't feel any passion whatsoever in my current role, and that's been hard to cope with. But through therapy I have discovered that having this job that I DON'T have emotional ties to is actually really good for me, and I am able to take all of my passion and drive and put it into my business on the side, which I hope can replace my full-time income one day.

I cant take time off, but if I’m working 5 12 hr shifts in a row, every hour off the clock is spent recovering. I try to sleep 8hrs, I eat breakfast, if I have time I try to go to the gym. If I’m doing 5 day 12hr shifts multiple weeks in a row, my 2 days off is errands and prepping for the next 5 days. It’s kinda horrible, and I had to squeeze time in with friends in small bites, but if I’m doing that, I’m in survival mode and unfortunately my mental and physical health takes priority 😭. If I burn out I lose empathy for patients and I am terrified of being that kind of health care worker. Take care of yourself before others when you can

I’ve known a ton of teachers (in the US) and it’s disgusting how we don’t compensate them for how much effort they have to put in. I get that not every teacher is amazing but I also feel like you’d retain better talent if the schools would just pay them way better.

Or just pay people what they’re worth and stop abusing their good intentions and separate the hard workers from the scrubs that for some reason always seem to move up on the back of your hard work. Burnout is a problem that employers created because they completely suck at doing their jobs which makes it harder for us to do ours. While continuing this illusion of “company culture” while the goal is just to make money and not eat pizza all the time. I would argue it’s a wider systemic issue as well because as the middleclass constantly gets destroyed do to Washington’s spending/money laundering problem burnout keeps growing and growing. My advice. Take a trade that can enable you to either be your own boss or get the experience that makes you to valuable to lose. Therefore you get to low key dictate terms with employers that don’t know what they are doing.

I had burnout from my studies during the great plague. I had everything set up before that, but during lockdown my ADHD symptoms really became exacerbated, and it went downhill. Especially since I was studying nursing and went from one extreme of no social contact, to being out in the field and having to engage with patients regularly while I was simultaneously struggling with managing symptoms. It was hard. My usual coping strategies weren’t available. I lost my structured schedule that I needed to maintain sanity. Going to the gym regularly was so important. Playing music with others was super important to reload my battery, and just disappeared overnight. I was faced with only my worst struggles. And by the time I had to quit I was just exhausted.

First thank you for having this channel to help educate people!! I deal with depression and anxiety, among other things, but after watching a couple of your videos about emotional burnout some things I am currently dealing with make more sense now. I do already have a plan to take a stay home vaccation after I get my room completely finished, and I have already set some good boundaries, but a vaccation is needed, just wish I could do so at the beach. Still, I do think a good week of nothing except doing things just for myself is exactly what is needed and will help to reset and recharge. Again thank you for having this channel to help educate people! Dealing with mental illness isn't easy and when family and friends don't fully understand it only adds to the problem. It's not always easy to find info that is easy to understand for the average person who doesn't understand all the technical terms either, but your videos explain things in a simple and easy to understand manner plus you have some visual content that is very helpful as well! I hope many people will watch these videos and that the stigma of mental illness will soon disappear as well as the fear and people being bullied and/or made fun of and discriminated simply because they deal with mental illness. Thank you!

I see a lot of people joking about getting fired or they couldn't take a break every 45 minutes. I used to have the same reaction when a therapist told me this years ago. However, I eventually tried it back then and still use it now. The type of job you have will mean you may have to be creative with how you take a break. For example when i worked in retail i couldn't go anywhere every hour. Instead I would take 5 mins every hour to be mindful. How's my breathing, drink some water, stretch. I work in an office now I still try to do the same thing. But now it includes a walk around the building, chatting with a coworker, stepping outside for fresh air. 5 mins is all I allow myself until lunch. So all jokes aside try it for a day or two it will help.

Giả thiết là có những người có sức khỏe tâm lý tinh thần khi sinh ra đã yếu, dễ nhạy cảm với những vấn đề khác nhau trong cuộc sống hơn người bình thường. Khi họ mệt mỏi vì cảm thấy bản thân không thể đáp ứng được những đòi hỏi về nghĩa vụ, trách nhiệm, áp lực từ gia đình, xã hội hay đơn giản chỉ là cảm thấy bị hút cạn năng lượng khi tiếp xúc với những người tiêu cực, họ tâm sự với những người họ tin tưởng là họ đã mệt mỏi rồi, nhưng những người đó không giống họ, người ta không thể hiểu vì sao họ lại có những suy nghĩ, nhận thức, cảm xúc, phản ứng như thế với những điều quá đỗi đơn giản như vậy. Như một vòng lặp vô nghĩa, người mang gánh nặng tâm lý lại tiếp tục cảm thấy không được cảm thông, tha thứ, thấu hiểu, tôn trọng dù họ đã nỗ lực phơi bày, lại cảm thấy bản thân khác biệt, lập dị, yếu đuối, vô năng, làm buồn lòng những người thân yêu. Không chỉ là cần được lắng nghe, người có tâm lý yếu thực sự cần ở thế giới bên ngoài là sự đồng tình, động viên, bao dung và tha thứ khi cần thiết. Điều lạc quan đó là bệnh tâm lý có thể biến mất khi tinh thần người bệnh trở nên mạnh mẽ hơn, khi tìm lại được sức mạnh bên trong do những niềm tin tiêu cực đã bị thay thế bởi niềm tin khác tích cực, đó là một quá trình dài bắt buộc cần tới sự giúp đỡ. Mong chúng ta sẽ không bao giờ vô tâm hay qua loa với những lời bộc bạch của một trái tim mang thương khi tổn tìm tới mình.

Big emphasis on finding a hobby. The last time I was majorly burned out, I made a list of the things I needed from my job to feel fulfilled and happy. Then I highlighted what I wasn’t getting, and sought those things through my hobbies. Now, I know that a hobby doesn’t cure burn out — it just helps a person to manage it better, and builds up resilience. Burnout is indicative of a company issue. But diversifying where we get those things on our list can really help!

I think it's also important to note that sometimes what we like as a hobby may not necessarily count as rest and self-care. For example, I may love sewing, knitting, crafting but that may also feel like a project. And if so, it's not self-care. The rest in the self-care must feel restful, more like play and do nothing, to reset. Thank you for your videos. I too work in the mental health profession and relate, and I like and appreciate what you do.

Lesson from the video: Almost 98% of people suffer; happiness is the unusual. Yet we overreact to our suffering. Our lives are like an onion, peeling away one layer only to reveal another. We think that once we overcome one problem, it's over. But life is like an onion, and we keep dealing with it. So no pain lasts forever. We're sad, we go out to talk to people, to confide in them—why? To realize that everyone else is suffering, in pain like us. We listen to their advice, and then what? When we return home, we still haven't addressed the root cause of the pain we're carrying. We love 10 people, and we break up with them all. Yes, this person has this reason, that person has that reason; we give 10 reasons to 10 people. So what? Put them on a piece of paper, and there's only one reason: past psychological pain. That pain from the heart is hard to heal, but it's not impossible. Once we know the reason, we find a way to solve it. I'm selfish, wanting to protect my loved ones from falling, not allowing them to stumble. But they need to learn their own lessons from that experience. It requires a lot of practice.

Burnout is more than being tired, it feels like your body forgot how to recover. I remember sitting at my desk, staring at the screen for hours, and getting nothing done. Even weekends didn’t recharge me. What really shifted things was Reset Your Body and Mind by Morgan Lane. It’s a 30-day reset that helped me find balance without making me feel guilty for slowing down. I didn’t realize how much better life could feel until I gave it a try. If you’re feeling stuck in that cycle, just begin with one small step. The change builds faster than you expect.

Careful with that metaphor if you're in autistic burnout. Vacation sounds like stress (the travel, the new place). Instead, think about what if for a certain time, you would have no obligations, all your needs would be taken care of by sb else than yourself and you could just focus on the things you love doing most for a while. See if that relaxes you or makes you cry in longing, then you may be exhausted.

Burnout is easy to miss IMO because it's not just one thing, it's the result of longterm stress over many little things that by themselves aren't enough to complain about. And eventually we get overwhelmed. "Gotta go to work, oh I slept bad last night so the morning routine is harder. Missed the buss by just a minute. The customer has a billion tiny things they wanna change in the project last minute. The customer doesn't speak the native language well so it's hard to even communicate what they want. The boss now needs me to prioritize another task that needs to be finished today. I'm worried of all the other tasks I'm falling behind on now. I mess up one of the tasks so my colleagues have to correct my mistake. Now I'm tired and falling behind on personal projects in my free time as well. Family and friends are badgering me because I didn't call them lately. Tried to squeeze in some gaming but there's so little time for it that I end up staying up too late." Rinse and repeat until you're fed up with it.

Earlier this year, I started medical residency. As soon as I noticed the signs of burnout, I told the team that I would need to anticipate my vacation, even if it was only 1 week. The boss denied my request, and went on to say that in the old days, the work was much harder than it is today, and said that he didn't think first-year residents should even take vacations. After 4 months, burnout turned into depression and I gave up on the program, on my dream. Today, I look back and see that just 1 week away from work, just playing Candy Crush in my room, could have prevented months of anhedonia, suicidal thoughts, feelings of worthlessness... I hope that in the near future there will be a greater awareness of burnout.

Sorry, but no, it’s not just people’s anxiety that’s the problem, it’s also the world we live in. It’s actual bosses getting on your case for even needing an unscheduled bathroom break or as you said, checking mouse movement or not allowing someone a mental break after losing their unborn child because “it’s not that big of a deal”. That’s not healthy. It is not a situation conducive for flexible scheduling and taking things slower. I think it’s inappropriate to suggest accountability is most often on employees and it’s just something they imagine when there’s on a societal level very much work needed on what’s considered acceptable and sustainable work environments for said employees. I think the examples being given might come from people in privileged positions who have the room to control their own schedules and have enough financial comfort to take things slower. That’s not to say it’s not good to strive for these things were you can, but this video felt rather dismissive of very real struggles people face that contribute to burnout.

I got burned out almost 18 months ago and I am STILL noticing the effects of this now (on and off). Two of my best friends also had burnout within the last year. Hard workers are especially at risk; the GUILT I felt when I finally took time off was unreal. It's so so hard. And when you're in the middle of it you are incapable of asking for help anymore. And employers totally exploit this.

The thing that finally worked for me was getting out of my head and into my body. Cold water does that instantly. You cannot think about your email inbox when you are sitting chest deep in 3 degree water. I facilitate breathwork and cold exposure sessions and the people who come in the most burned out are usually the ones who have the biggest breakthroughs. Your nervous system needs a hard reset sometimes and thinking your way through burnout rarely works.

This is fascinating. I often hear and feel that "2 day weekends are simply not enough rest", which, when they are your ONLY days you give yourself permission to rest, they aren't. However, this 7 day work week kind of unlocks the idea that you CAN rest 7 days a week BECAUSE you are also applying conscientious effort towards work or goals. The 2 days of rest into Monday "catch up" is setting up for failure, but only a couple hours of effort in those same 2 days makes both the days feel better, AND the work week feel less stressful. Truly a win-win.

Though it started as a religious thing for me, the weekly Jewish Sabbath for me has become my antidote for burnout. I'd recommend that anybody apply some of the rules of a Sabbath to their own life. From sunset Friday until sundown Saturday, I'm forbidden from doing any work, chores, commerce, or attending most major events. Though most focus on what I don't get to do on these days, but I focus on what I don't have to do on those days. I sleep in, spend time with those close in my community, read, go for walks, or simply just nap. Work isn't on my mind because I literally am not allowed to touch it. It's probably my favorite day of the week. You don't have to be Jewish to observe a weekly Sabbath. 👍

In 2009 I was an IT Director for a private college in the Bay Area. My boss, the CFO was fired for political reasons, and because he hired me, they were trying to make my life miserable so I'd quit. I had been working 12-18 hour days for months because things were so screwed up. I went for an annual physical, and my doctor asked me how work was going. I started crying and couldn't stop. He called in a Psychologist, and I was put on disability. A few months later, I was "laid off" while on disability. I was just so happy to be free that I didn't care about the legality. It wasn't the workload that was the issue. It was the fact that I was working my ass off, and not only wasn't it appreciated by senior leadership, but they were actively trying to undermine me. If you want the best work from your teams, ensure you care for them and that they feel cared for. Burnout is real.

I’m 24 years old, moved to America 3 years ago and got married to my best friend and took over the responsibilities of taking care of his elderly parents with Parkinson’s in there 80s and that was my wanting. I also live in a 5000sq feet house and have a ocd and want the house constantly clean. I also take care of three cats and 10 chicken including a garden full of veggies and flowers. I am also going to the gym recently to grow more muscle and stretch my body, thinking it would help with my overall health. I started getting gray hair lately and I now I’m totally burned out, but I have to push myself. I cook three meals a day for my parents in laws, low carb, low protein, high in veggies. My husband is at work and when he comes back he himself has a lot of stuff on his mind, i don’t want to dumb more stress on to him. Yet I feel like I am about to lose myself. The responsibility of being a full time caregiver and housewife is already hard and I also have to worry about political issues because I don’t have a green card. I don’t have any female friends, because I don’t have time to make any. My living space became my work environment, I constantly have to keep talking positiv “you can do it, push yourself, just gotta clean the cat toilette, take out the trash, feed the chicken, cook lunch, clean the kitchen, put away the groceries, clean out the pantry, feed the cats, check on parents in law, water the plant, repot the plants. if I push myself I get it all done in 4 hours and I can rest, I can do it, it isn’t that much” but who am I kidding, I can’t. I feel like I am all alone, I feel empty, I don’t feel happy anymore. Nothing is exciting, nothing is real and this isn’t even everything. There is so much more I have to deal with, I haven’t seen my family in 2 years since I can’t travel outside of the country until I get my green card. I am not suicidal saying this but I feel like I have lost the meaning to my life. I just exist. Maybe if I stop thinking of being a person and start living like a robot everything would be easier. I can’t really go anywhere for longer than 4 hours, afraid that my mother in law will get out of bed and fall, she is fall prone and has also dementia. Forget holidays, forget even taking off a day because I can’t find any care givers around me. This is one of the best explanations of how the nervous system gets stuck in survival mode. What I see constantly in the people I work with is exactly what you describe here. The body keeps running the threat response long after the actual threat is gone, and most people have no idea that is what is happening. They think they are broken or that something is wrong with them when really their nervous system is doing exactly what it was designed to do. It just needs to be shown that it is safe to come back down. Breathwork and cold exposure are two of the most direct ways I have found to retrain that response. Not by thinking about it but by giving the body a physical experience of moving through discomfort and coming out the other side calm. Thank you for making this so clear and accessible.

Kinda sad to read all the comments from people dismissing the suggestions in the video, seemingly without trying any of them. If you think your job is always going to suck and be overwhelming and you just have to deal with it, of course you're going to get burnt out. You get to choose whether you stay in a shitty situation and do nothing to change it, or you choose to try different things to fix the problem. These suggestions aren't guaranteed to work for everyone, you have to look at the idea and brainstorm ways to use it in your specific circumstances. Yes, there are certain outside forces that you can't do anything to change or work around them, but I'd argue if you're watching this video you're not in a powerless situation.

It's nice to hear this however, for most of this doesn't help. Most of us work to survive. we don't have the luxury of choosing another occupation or to take time off, or even in my case, afford any type of counseling. I work 50-56 hours a week in order just to make ends meet regardless if I'm burned out or not, regardless of I'm depressed or not. I know there are people who work more or work 2 jobs in order to maintain their lives financially. In this day and age, it's a matter of survival. Contentment and mental wellbeing doesn't enter the picture. Those are luxuries not afforded to us.

If you work a standard 9-5 and you need to do your work within those times, you can't do this. My point being, the only time you can really do this is if you're lucky enough to have work that does not have strict restrictions on time and days you work, and has that breakup between different kinds of tasks. You, Dr. K, are in a very privileged position to be able to create the kind of work week you wish to have, and adopt to your needs. Many people lack that position. The video lacks that sensitivity, which can feel quite sad to those people who can not break up their days like you. That said, i highly appreciate your input and this is definitely still very valuable. I have experienced the idea that not all work is equal, and that different times and different tasks tend to feel better or worse depending on the combination. I am greatful for having a position at my work that is a bit more freeing to let me reorganize my tasks, although i am not enough so to be able to change on what days i do what tasks. For anyone reading that's not in a position to do that, i sympathize and i hope you can view this as a way to look at work or necessary things to do in life outside of work, as this information can still be helpful there. Thank you Dr.K and the community. You are all awesome <3

I think some folks are missing the point a bit. Burnout and stress are both internal feelings, you can have them whether you are hitting goals at work or trying and failing. If you can't manage the stress you have from work, it may be time for a new job. Your employer cares about results and reliability, but your job is to care about YOU. If you are suffering, no metric will make that job feel good. The advice in this video is about remembering every person has agency and can be an active participant in living a healthier life. I could do a great or average job at work and still be laid off. Either way, I should avoid burnout so I can be healthier whether I'm at one workplace or another.

I've been experiencing this a lot lately. I put in extreme effort with minimal returns. I work in the world of assessment for disabilities in children and how they impact school functioning. I cannot tell you how disheartening it is to work for 2 months on a case (while juggling daily emergencies and another 20+ cases at the same time) just to be met with anger, distrust, snide comments, witch-hunting behavior, and possible legal action whether I write a 10 page report or a 40 page report about a child's functioning and needs for a caregiver or a colleague. Usually it's because they want some sort of a magic fix that removes the disability, they think there's something being hidden from them (like a magical placement or cure for Dyslexia), or that the findings are just wrong even if it's all based on data and statistical evidence and is corroborated by everyone involved in the assessment process of that child. Then I go home and play games and feel much better and know it's not my fault but I have to go through it on a weekly basis.

Minh sinh ra trong một gia đình bình thường ở một thị trấn nhỏ. Ngoại hình và học lực của mình rất bình thường. Mình chưa bao giờ bị áp lực bởi điểm số, thành tích, cũng chưa bao giờ biết cố gắng là gì. Bố mẹ không đặt áp lực cho mình và ngay cả bản thân mình cũng không tự tạo động lực hay áp lực cho bản thân. Mình không có ước mơ, việc thi lên lớp hay chuyển cấp của mình diễn ra rất suôn sẻ, có lẽ là do may mắn nên mình luôn thi đỗ vào những lớp tốt nhất của trường (c2, c3). Thi tốt nghiệp cũng không làm mình lo lắng, mình không vùi đầu vào giải đề như người ta, mình luôn bị thầy cô “ghim” vì đã dốt lại còn lười. Tuy học lớp chọn nhưng thành tích của mình cũng coi như gần bét rồi😂. Mình biết như vậy là không hề tốt cho tương lai nhưng mình không thể sửa nổi. Mình đã từng quyết tâm nhưng hôm sau vẫn chứng nào tật nấy, lười và dốt dần đều. Mình không bất ngờ với điểm thi đại học, bằng đúng với hai lần thi thử của mình luôn. Lúc điền nguyện vọng, mình không biết mình thích gì nhưng biết là mình ghét toán nên mình đăng kí đúng những ngành không phải học toán. Thế rồi mình đã đưa ra một quyết định mà ai cũng phản đối, đến mình còn thấy khó tin. Điều ấy làm mình của hiện tại chìm sâu vào bể bế tắc… Không chỉ khó mà mình vẫn luôn giữ thái độ học tập như ở c3 nên thành tích vẫn cứ là giảm dần đều và có dấu hiệu trượt môn. Mình rất hối hận và muốn thay đổi thực sự, nhưng không biết bắt đầu từ đâu😢…

I found something interesting about myself and maybe it might help fellow neurodivergent asd folks. Whenever I burn out from my job or talking to other people, I try to take breaks, watch movies, read, but it never makes me truly relaxed and doesn't let me let go of the stress inertia. But I found that if I specifically engage with my special interests, it truly lets me become relaxed and allows me to recover

I have been told by my psychiatrist that I'm burned out. Since I have clinical depression with anhedonia traits it all seemed the same to me. I never actually thought much of it - the lack of energy, poor performance, being utterly uninterested about my job, and that constant "eh" feeling about everything. I was a little shocked to learn some of my symptoms were caused by actually being burned out. And not my depression per se. Everyone, please be attentive to what you are feeling and don't invalidate your struggle. And reach out for help when you need it, you might feel like you don't, but that is actually the problem.

I’m not sure I want to literally block out all of my time each week with a calendar. Something feels robotic and unhuman about it. I think we shouldn’t just fly by the seat of our pants but this advise seems like a way to cope with the system instead of pulling back from it. Most people will notice when they begin to set boundaries and resist even just a little they will be threatened with their livelihood being taken away, or they’ll be warned that they aren’t being a “team player.” The reason they can do this is because they have us by our throats. The job we have is our lifeline and getting another one often isn’t easy or quick. It can bankrupt you to resist even a little. The owners know this and use it to keep you obedient and productive. What’s weird is when you stop and ask yourself, “what the hell am I even producing?”

Thank you! I feel so guilty about being burnt out even though I'm just a stay-at-home parent. I told this a therapist once and he stopped me right there, pointing out that I don't get to leave my job site and that's hard in its own, valid way. My husband is burnt out as well, especially since he's been doing a lot of overtime, so I try to do all the cooking, housework, and most of the parenting. The issue is that as a neurodivergent person I've struggled with normal life even before I became a parent, but potentially having to give up on my lifelong big family dream hurts too. But yeah, it seems like more than either of us can handle. I try to take things one step at a time for now because I don't want to deal with more, but I'm no good at that either. I don't know where I'm going with this anymore and I think I'm just pushing off getting chores done and going to bed, so I'll stop. I'm too tired to focus anyways. Somehow I was very confused when watching this. Questions I had immediately was "wait, what's the deadline of these tasks? What if you don't know what the topic is going to be before they told you to write a lecture in 6 hours? What if you didn't have a choice to do a certain stupid task any other day than the day the boss told you to do it? How is it you have room to plan your work like this?" Then I realized; this video doesn't apply to people with a regular job. Regular jobs have strict deadlines, and usually piss poor management. You couldn't do all the stuff Dr K. is talking about in a regular job. It seems that while Dr K. still works for people to a degree, he has a lot more control over his topics and deadlines, room to stretch, if you will. Not so in most other places. I think this would apply better for people doing independent work, or at least jobs that have a high level of autonomy, both of which are *very* privileged positions. I don't see this video helping most people.

For the longest time I thought burnout just meant “being tired.” But when it hit me, I realized it’s way deeper, it’s when your brain feels foggy, your body is drained, and even things you used to love feel empty. I kept blaming myself, thinking I just needed more discipline, but the truth is I was stuck in survival mode. What helped me wasn’t pushing harder, but giving myself permission to reset. I tried a 30-day plan that focused on energy, sleep, and focus in realistic steps, and slowly I started feeling like myself again. Mornings didn’t feel so heavy, and I could finally enjoy small things without guilt. If burnout feels like your “normal,” don’t ignore it. The guide that helped me was Reset Your Body and Mind by Morgan Lane, and it honestly gave me the structure I needed to recover instead of sinking deeper.

As an healthcare/SEND coordinator, I get asked about burnout a lot. If you're passionate and into your job/career then burnout isnt as likely as if you're doing something just to get by. I LOVE the satisfaction of getting a child with severe needs the right person to work with them, knowing that I'm only called on once all other options are exhausted and there is nothing more fulfilling than working on a child for a month and then speaking to the parents about how well they're getting on now. 7am - 6pm Mon - Fri, 12 - 5pm most Saturdays (I really do enjoy my work. My partner doesn't even mind because I'm tired but not burnt after I finish, and I do still find time for the gym. Work a job to make ends meet sure, but work on yourself to find yourself in a job that you find fulfilment in. If that means working in the local to make ends meet while you study then do it. The sooner you get that out the way, the sooner you can start living the life you want

My partner, the love of my life, who is and has been everything to me for my healing and growth, and I went on a break almost a month ago because of how shaky the ground is right now, and I'm not sure where that's going to end up, and it's devastating me. I know I just have to stay present with myself, but I feel so dysregulated all day and I'm just so freaking burnt out of all of it... except for friends. Going to lunch and doing activities with them has been a lifeline I never knew I needed so badly. I've found almost a calling or purpose from pouring into others. I have been leaning on them for support for the past several weeks and it's been so healing for me to have such wonderful people in my life, helping me get through this really tough time. I'm still just...so exhausted from it all.

Definitely agree that burnout leads to becoming fragile and less resilient to times of being pushed too hard, whether by yourself, others, or whatever life throws at you. I'm still overcoming burnout, and personally for me the crashes come from taking things a bit too easy to try to recover or by me making the same mistakes that seemed so harmless before like putting in that extra hour or two of work, or putting in more effort throughout the day so that I can try to get ahead. It sounds counter intuitive, but my solution is to put the brakes on being an over achiever. Like with anyone, being an overachiever led to a lot of success for me earlier. I got addicted to it but did it to much to the point where I literally have to re-train my brain to what a "normal" amount of daily work is again to fix what I got going on. Some of the weird struggles I face right now is trying to find that sweet spot of not taking things too easy with work to where it puts me in a low energy and lethargic state of being, but not working too much either. Like sometimes I have a hard time figuring out whether the times I crash are from either me taking things way too lathargically easy or from the times I unknowingly pushed myself too hard. Being consistent with the recovery isnt always easy, but it is getting easier over time and I have no doubt I'll get over it eventually. It's just pretty annoying in the meantime. Thought I would just share my experience in case others might find it useful because it took me forever to pin down exactly what my problems and solutions are just because of how paradoxical the nature of it all is.

This video clearly explained how burnout isn’t just being tired - it’s a mix of emotional, mental, and even physical exhaustion. I could feel that in my own life. I’ve been pushing myself hard - staying up late, taking on extra things because I felt like I had to prove I was good enough. But the video pointed out that what we need isn’t more hustle - it’s rest, balance, and clear boundaries. It made me realize pushing through burnout by working harder is exactly what creates more burnout. Now I’m trying to set limits: I’m saying no more often, scheduling real breaks, and trying to sleep earlier. It’s been a shift, but I already feel lighter. The takeaway is that you don’t have to prove anything to anyone - sometimes the strongest thing you can do is step back and rest.

balance and understanding that yes nothing will last forever, emotions fluctuate for multiple reasons yes life happens but it’s about choice, it’s about choosing that this right here is worth pouring I’ve been noticing lately how much self-judgments that push me to go against my natural energy are making things harder for me. I left a community not long ago that I outgrew, and I’m currently in a season of venturing out to some new communities and finding new mentors to continue growing in a couple areas that are important to me. Now that I’m meeting a lot of new people, I’m bumping into my own temperament of being slow to warm up to people and needing a lot of energy to process new people and places, until I become comfortable opening up. I noticed all these self-judgments came online about why can’t I grow up and be comfortable just talking to new people, or why can’t I push through a bunch of new experiences instead of having to rest a bunch in between. I realized that these judgments were making it even harder and more exhausting being around people. I’m trying now to lean into accepting the pace that works for me and seeing if I can accept that I might need and want to observe and hang back for a while before I open up to people. Looking to see if I could do that with a more calm energy rather than an energy of self-judgment.

From my mine of work and other in it I've talked to. If you have over bearing supervisors that treat you poorly you burn out faster. It is often called Retired On duty. But if your boss treats you fairly and shows they care and has your back, engages in ideas it happens less often. A study was done on factory workers. The lighting was poor. So the factory bosses tried to fix the lights to increase productivity. After three or four lighting improvements work increased each time. They asked why the workers had improved. And the consensus was "W e complained about the lighting and the company did something." Shocked, because they thought it was the natural lighting and a softer light that was better the fluorescence. But showing you care about employees is the most helpfull.

It’s crazy how burnout doesn’t just feel like “being tired”, it feels like your brain and body are permanently stuck in survival mode. For me, it was endless scrolling at night, zero focus during the day, and this weird numbness where even rest didn’t feel like rest. I honestly thought something was broken in me. The turning point was stumbling on Reset Your Body and Mind by Morgan Lane. It wasn’t some rigid “discipline” thing it was a gentle 30-day reset that actually worked in real life. I didn’t realize how much energy and clarity I was missing until I felt it again. You really don’t know what you’re leaving on the table until you try. If you’re in that burnout loop, just start with Day 1. Give yourself the reset. You’ll feel the shift sooner than you expect.

The word burnout triggers a lot of fear in me. I have OCD and AuDHD, so when concepts are described vaguely or abstractly my brain tends to spiral around them. What I struggle to understand is: what actually is burnout? The way it’s sometimes described online can make it sound like something that appears almost suddenly - like one day you hit a wall and your energy never really comes back. That idea honestly scares me. One of my biggest fears is suddenly losing the level of energy and drive I’m used to having, ending up bed-bound, developing something like chronic fatigue, and never really returning to baseline. When I read about burnout, that’s often the mental picture my brain creates — even though that might not be what people actually mean. Ironically, worrying about burnout probably increases stress and cortisol in itself. Another layer for me is that I live with severe misophonia and constant hypervigilance, so my nervous system rarely gets a real sense of “off”. Relaxation doesn’t come naturally. I’ve tried many treatments and medications without any real effect (currently only on sertraline). Stimulants help with ADHD, but they don’t affect the hypervigilance or misophonia. Because of that, what actually keeps me functioning - and honestly keeps me sane with the misophonia - is momentum. Grinding, staying engaged, moving forward, chasing dopamine in constructive ways. That “go-go-go” energy is what keeps me from collapsing under the constant stress my nervous system already lives in. Sitting and watching a movie would spike my pulse far more than hyperfocusing on something and distracting myself. It’s sad, but it’s how I survive. So when burnout is described as something that could suddenly remove that energy or drive, it feels terrifying — because that drive is also what helps me cope with something that is otherwise almost unbearable. So my genuine question is: how should someone realistically understand burnout? Is it typically a gradual process with warning signs, or can it genuinely feel like it appears suddenly? Also just wanted to say that I really like your videos and the way you explain these topics.🙏🙏

I have had the opportunity of taking almost 6 sabbatical years in my life (and I'm not even 30), not because I wanted, but because I was basically forced to take them due to my "outstanding" health... I know that not many people are able to take them and most are forced to keep studying and working and praying that they don't drop dead from exhaustion any day, but still somehow I feel very "behind" in life due to barely being able to finish university and with almost zero work experience, yet most people my age (mid to late 20s) are starting to reach "milestones" such as having a stable job, a business, a proper career, buying houses, cars, being able to afford nicer things. I know that I cannot overwork myself unless I want to die somewhat young or maybe middle aged, so I know I have to seek a career that allows me to have a modest lifestyle while not wearing myself down. Because I know if I did a demanding job, eventually I will have to spend all the money earned in hospital bills.

I'm so burned out that I'm abandoning friendships in a desperate attempt to have more time for myself. But people just expect so much. People at work want to hang out afterwards. People outside of work want to infringe upon my decompression time once work is over. People assume that my weekends are free for socialising, when I need them to recover enough energy to lather, rinse & repeat the next week. Honestly, if I never interacted in-person with another human being in my life, it would be too fucking soon.

Mình giờ cũng gần 40 tuổi rồi.Giờ cha mẹ mình cũng gần như lặp lại sai lầm của bà.Là ko kiếm việc làm cho mình vì cho rằng mình ko làm dc gì hết và ko cho mình đi đâu xa hoặc ko cho mình đi chỗ nào đông xe vì sợ mình gặp nguy hiểm.Mình ít khi nào tâm sự với cha mẹ mình vì có nói đôi khi bị phản bác lại nếu mình nói gì ko vừa ý.Và cha mình sẵn sàng chê bai mình trước mặt đông người nếu mình làm gì ko vừa ý.Người lớn đôi khi ko hoàn hảo nhưng đôi khi phải nhìn vào cảm xúc của con mình.Chứ ko phải cái kiểu muốn tốt hơn cho con mình nên muốn sắp đặt sao thì muốn

Been a developer for 20 years. Hit major burnout three times. It's absolutely a real thing and will build up unbreakable walls. I will stare at my computer for hours at a time without a single keystroke. Its like a mental battlefield and you will lose most battles. Each burnout period took roughly three months for me to break out of and generally cost one job in the process. I'm currently in my third burnout. And I think this might be my breaking point. I'm nearing the point of dropping my 20 year career and it breaks my heart. Burnout will not only affect what is causing the burnout, like a job, but every other aspect of your life. If anyone else is burnt out and suffering, know you are not alone.
