Ugh burnout. I experienced burnout during the pandemic as people were laid off and no one to replace them. In turn, I did double the work and almost doubled my hours to complete said work. On top of it, I was going to school part time. I attempted to address it with my boss and was told, everyone is going through it and sometimes we have to give up what we want to get by. So, I had to drop my classes to carry this workload. As time went on my workload increased. I did eventually have a mental breakdown. My point is because I never successfully addressed it, I developed gastritis and what my doctor said was gastroparesis this past January. It's now the end of September and I just recovered from the gastritis and a little bit of the gastroparesis has resolved. I'm thinking I'll be 100 percent by the end of the year. Address burnout because not only can burnout manifest itself mentally it can manifest physically. The first few months of coming down with this were scary, I had no clue what was happening to my body and lost 15 pounds.

I kept wondering if I was depressed. I wasn’t sad just tired, unmotivated, numb. Turns out, it wasn’t depression. It was burnout from constant overstimulation. Reset Your Body and Mind by Morgan Lane helped me see the difference and more importantly, heal from it. That 30-day reset brought my energy, sleep, and focus back things I didn’t even realize I lost. If you’re not sure what’s wrong, start with a reset. You’ll feel the shift.

I actually quit my job 9 months ago because of burnout. I actually liked what I did and I was good at it. But that feeling being exhausted all the time, never having energy for myself and family, I couldn't deal with it any more. I have no regrets leaving that job. I would absolutely do that work again, I would even work at the same place, but the time away has given me time to realise I just didn't have the capacity to do everything that was asked of me at work and still have something left in "the tank" for home and myself. That word capacity is a big one I got to understand this year. There are people who have the capacity to do what I did and still have energy after for their personal lives, me definitely not. So now I'm looking for work in the same area, but part time instead of full time. I'm looking forward to finding a good work/life balance.

A few points though: 1. you can do worst tasks early in the week or as soon as it appears. It's called "eating the frog". I've been in burnout many times, and I think this current one started about 4-5 months ago. But life is extremely stressful at the moment, so there is no rest for a while yet, I have no choice but to push through, again. Next week I will ask my doctor to send me to get a diagnosis for CPTSD, because it seems like many of my bodily issues can be explained by that, and as one fellow said, "If you grew up with undiagnosed autism and decades of trauma at home, in school and in the workplace, how can you NOT have CPTSD?" When I got out of a 16 year depression a few years back, I thought I'd solved life, I knew how to handle it, boy was I wrong. It was a nice vacation, and now I will get back to work on myself, armed with more knowledge and confidence, and hopefully some professional help🤞

I’ve held this job for 15 years and have reported to the same director, who has no desire to develop her direct reports. Everyone reporting to her is basically there to help her succeed in her role. She continually hires external people to fill senior roles and does not promote anyone existing in our division into these roles. The only way up, is to leave. I guess I’ve had enough of my job, but I feel afraid to leave and start over.

What they don't tell you is how long it will take your brain to recover from it. I'm 2 yrs out from a particularly bad case of burnout that I was compelled by my life circumstances to ignore, and honestly I don't know if I will ever fully be my old self again when it comes to productivity.

I used to wake up tired, drag myself through the day, and crash on the couch at night feeling like I’d accomplished nothing — even though my mind was constantly racing. Coffee didn’t help anymore, my sleep was broken, and I felt like I was aging twice as fast. I knew I was burnt out but didn’t know where to start fixing it. Then I found this book with a 30-day plan that didn’t ask for perfection — just small, daily shifts to reset how I sleep, eat, and deal with stress. Honestly, it helped me reconnect with my body in a way I hadn’t in years. If anyone feels stuck in that same fog, this is the book that helped me: Reset Your Body and Mind by Morgan Lane.

This was very helpful. Thank you so much. I understand the concept and myself so much better now. I've had burnout for the majority of my life from C-PTSD from an extremely young age. I've noticed my whole family actually deals with burnout. I was getting so much better, SO much better. It only took me 3-4 months to recover (not fully) from a severely traumatic event that happened before I turned 16. Then, six months later, I got severely injured from a car accident and had to live on a recliner for 4-5 months. It's been about 6 months since the car accident.. but today, I am also affected by the severe traumas of my early and mid childhood with flashbacks, night terrors, severe dissociation. Not to mention, I'm most likely dealing with a nervous breakdown again. This is my later "childhood" I'm assuming? I have won the "unhealthiest 17 year old, physically and mentally award" Yay me! Nerve damage (feet, hands, back), bulged discs, circulation problems, hypothyroid, post-concussive syndrome, bulimia...etc. I won! *cough, *cough *BURNOUT. Anyone out there that feels alone, I just want to say that you are strong, you're definitely not alone, and give it time. You're life is craving a break. I highly recommend a healthy diet and even just a LITTLE exercise every now and then. I understand how hard it can be to even eat healthy food when burned out, yet exercise? Is this chick crazy? And reach out for talk therapy, and your compassionate, loving friends. Hell, get a dog you can talk to, but don't do it in public and don't tell anyone I told you to do that. Do some EASY stretching even. I promise you, it will make your day feel even .0001% better. Take care everyone. Hydrate! Laughter cures. Lessons from live 113: Exams are just a small part of life, so don’t put too much pressure on yourself. Learning is the easiest part. Learn to understand the global economic situation so you know where you stand—and invest in the right place, which is yourself. Focusing your inner strength on just three things in life is enough. Nothing is perfect. Ask yourself which role you truly want to strive for. Understand the level of awareness you are at so you can live in alignment with your own life—without judging yourself. Understand the left and right brain in order to make appropriate decisions and apply them correctly in daily life. When you gain the right knowledge, you should know how to share it so others can benefit as well.

Burnout doesn't happen just from a stressful work environment. Many people suffer burnout from caregiving or other situations. Many people experiencing burnout are overwhelmed in general in their lives. This pandemic worsened it because of the isolation and increased stress. That exhaustion that isn't relieved by sleep, feeling unmotivated and overwhelmed, depressed, disorganized. They are all part of burnout. And it can also make you physically I'll if it's bad enough. Everyone has a breaking point. We aren't machines and need time to rest our minds as well as our bodies to be healthy and happy. We need to talk to trained professionals who can give us good tools and coping strategies. Burnout doesn't just go away.

I thought burnout meant working 16 hours a day. But mine came from doing nothing all day — just mindless scrolling, bad sleep, zero energy, and no purpose. That’s when I found Reset Your Body and Mind by Morgan Lane and it felt like someone finally got what I was going through. If that sounds like you… this might be your turning point.

I worked as an MSP technician for 3 years for a small MSP. When I first started, I was green to the field, and had alot to prove, and learned alot quickly due to how we function and the employer took advantage of it. I was labeled as a desktop support technician, but my roles turned quickly into an MSP technician. After 2 years of powering through mini burnouts, and continuing to do more and more on top of my role, I finally realized how much bs it was. I realized I was the ONLY technician, when I was always thinking I had my boss to look toward for direction, when in fact, without any discussion, they set me by myself, including accountability. I hit my breaking point when I brought up my concerns about my work and the burnout I was feeling, and they lied to me stating they would help to make changes. It didn't happen. I just started a new job, making much more, doing much less. I felt so torn to leave as I spent so long, and felt I had so much responsibility. But in the end, it was either me, or the job. And even after two days of working where I am, I feel 1000% better.

I retired from the music industry in 2019 because of burnout. 14 hour work days, never a day off, waking up in a different country every day for weeks at a time, no time for friends or family and all for less money than I would have earned at McDonald's despite playing arenas in front of thousands of people. I became disillusioned, despondent and angry and started going down the alt-right rabbit hole. I didn't know at the time it was burnout. I couldn't bring myself to pick up my drumsticks anymore. I didn't want to be around music, and I felt like it was all pointless. For the past four years even something as small as someone suggesting I play or teach again has been painful. I'm finally able to put some words to my emotions after 3 years of therapy and then getting diagnosed with both ADHD and Autism. I'm starting to want to play again.

Always keep in mind that as a woman, you should aim higher, not settle lower, because it’s unlikely you’ll go back to loving someone poor.

I had 12 years of chaotic stress touching every aspect of my life. I've been able to pretty much eliminate those external stressors for the past 3 years. Now I emotionally handle stressors well, calm and clear headed while others around me are breaking down or crashing out. HOWEVER, my BODY does not handle them well. Ex: If I get an adrenaline surge the next day I get canker sores. Or recently I got notice that my income was going to go down. I knew it was an error and needed to (re)submit paperwork. I was emotionally calm, some worry (who wouldn't?), but no emotional outbursts or stuffing emotions. However for 5 days, including 3 days after it was resolved, my blood pressure was elevated. It feels like my body was damaged by those hard times which ironically made me mentally and emotionally resilient 😡

I found this video insightful. Certain foods would basically give me a migraine and make it really hard to think. Eventually I figured out that it was foods containing extra MSG that would cause this for me. The G in MSG is glutamate. Every time I bring this up, there are many people who do not believe me, but after watching this video, I feel validated that there is a scientific link discovered between glutamate and burnout. I don't know why it affects me and not other people. I don't mean to sound pretentious but my IQ is quite high and perhaps my brain just works differently from others and so glutamate has this effect on me. Maybe I produce more rent than others and so my tolerance for additional glutamate is less, or maybe my brain is not cleaning it out as effectively as others.

Burnout isn’t weakness, it’s the body whispering what the mind refuses to admit: you’ve been at war with your own biology. Cortisol is not the enemy, but a misunderstood messenger. When we stop glorifying constant output and learn to feel again, through rhythm, rest, and realignment, we begin the healing. The nervous system remembers peace, but we must choose it consciously.

I'm 64 years old, been stressed all my life, over weight and have type 2 diabetes. Recently I've been to coaching sessions to address my weight issue. What I discovered is somewhere in my troublesome childhood I took responsibility for everything! I was stressed all the time! This was my core value. I'm now putting my needs first and I've starting to think more about what I eat, not through self discipline, but easily through self love. Suddenly my job has become alien to me because I was in it to rescue situations. What's interesting is these six coaching sessions have been funded by the UK National Health Service, where I live, via a charity that helps people like me. Keep up your great work, there must be so many people out dying because of unnecessary self inflected stress. Thank you 😊

I've been experiencing burnout for as long as I can remember. There are so many skills I want to learn; I want to learn to draw, to speak multiple languages, and to build things. Every time I try to learn a new skill, I can practice it for 40 days in a row, well passed the point that it should become a habit, but on the 41st day I crash and burn so hard that I can't even pick it back up again for months or years. My family think I'm lazy, or that I don't actually like the thing I'm so desperately trying to learn; it's demoralizing that no one understands what I go through or won't help me. Last week my psychologist told me , based on some test results and a questionnaire, that I have ADHD, and he referred me to a psychiatrist that I will be meeting this Thursday. I don't know what will happen but maybe things will finally start getting better after this week.

Lol, my work exists out of a whole list of tasks which have subtasks. There is no reducing, as in: even in reality there is no reducing tasks. I've got a list with 45 cases on my name and when I worked on one I need to go to the other, that's how it's structured. They all have phases that require different actions. So I do case 1 with task A and go to case 2 with task C and case 3 with task B and case 4 with task E and case 5 with task B. It keeps on going endlessly. Oh and I get called in between as well. I can decide not to pick up, however I need to speak with the people from the case so not picking up can lead to having to call them again multiple times until they pick up one of these times. Or worse, needing to send an extra letter to establish contact, which is even more work. So usually, you pick up anyway, getting interrupted. Modern jobs will be a good form of punishment in hell as well. without vacation days or weekends 🙃 I will make sure to have some rest moments other then the 40 minutes midday break.

In my perspective, this song expresses what it's like to be in a long-term relationship, and slowly falling out in the process. May mga nagbago over the years. Yung taong minahal mo in the beginning, wala na. It might feel like you're both tip-toeing, nag-iingat, naiilang, mas naging cautious sa bawat actions at salita kasi baka yun na ang maging breaking point. Di na gaya ng dati na malaya nyong nasasabi ang gusto nyo nang walang takot. Ngayon, parang kaunting mali, away. Kaunting di pagkakaintindihan, pwedeng humantong sa hiwalayan. Ayaw nyo lang bumitaw kasi ang tagal nyo ring minahal ang isa't-isa. Sayang yung mga taon, kaya patuloy ninyong sinusubukang ayusin. I suffered clinical burnout, severe clinical depression and diagnosable adrenal fatigue from my job. Brought on by a toxic boss, working in a pressure cooker, and a hostile work environment. Coupled with a personal loss, and a work injury that resulted in severe chronic pain, that I had to learn how to absorb/sit in the pain because nothing worked to relieve it. I ended up struggling for another year, concentrated on eliminating my monthly outflow of cash (bills), paid off credit cards and cancelled them. And saved as much as I could. I was able to quit work for two months and then found a part time job. I would ride my motorcycle almost daily. Sleep as long as I wanted and just sit in my hammock and watch the birds for hours. It was bare bones living but I somewhat healed my body, mind and soul. That was 3 years ago. I still struggle when I feel the least bit stressed. It is a long road to recovery, of which I really don't know if I will ever completely recover from. I now try to live the most peaceful, calm, drama-free life I can. I have walked away from people and jobs to maintain my peace and contentment. Your mental health, people is one of your most precious resources.

I've been experience creative burn out for over a year now. I have a want to do list a mile long but just no motivation to do any of it. So to try and get out of my slump I am doing a sort of ADHD Take The Wheel challenge, where I am letting myself just do whatever gets me excited creatively for however long it holds that attention. I've been feeling so much better doing this and my motivation to create has climbed a ton by just not forcing myself unless it's for work. A part of this is forcing myself to not see incomplete projects as a failure. I'll get to it eventually again.

I was afraid of night because I knew I was not able to fall asleep, or even if I fell asleep, i would wake up 2 hours later feeling anxious about what was causing it, with tingling on my spinal cords pretty much every where on my body, sweating. Burnout is something you don’t notice until it’s already taken over your entire life. For me it wasn’t dramatic, it was slow. I stopped enjoying things I used to love, simple tasks felt overwhelming, and every day started to feel the same. I wasn’t sad, I wasn’t angry, I was just empty. What made it worse is that I kept telling myself to push harder, as if I could “motivate” my way out of it. But burnout isn’t about effort, it’s about running on an empty tank for too long. If this video feels way too familiar, don’t ignore it. I wish someone told me sooner that burnout is a real state, not a personality flaw. Reset Your Body and Mind by Morgan Lane helped me understand what was happening and how to slowly rebuild myself. It honestly gave me a starting point when I had none.

great resignation is fantastic response to be on that "verge of burnout" in my opinion. If you don't feel valued and appreciated, before you lose your own sense of value and let yourself be wounded, just runaway. You'll find someplace else, they'll find someone else. Nobody can be asked to sacrifice themselves or their health for a project. Because without your health you can't work and you'll have no income to put a roof over your head or food on your plate.

For years I thought changing jobs would fix my exhaustion. I kept believing that if I just got a “better” position or more recognition, I would feel alive again. But even when things seemed perfect on paper, I woke up drained, distracted, and emotionally flat. Weekends didn’t help, hobbies felt pointless, and I kept blaming myself for not being able to “handle it.” What really helped me was discovering a structured 30-day approach that focused on rebuilding energy, sleep, and focus without overwhelming me. It gave me practical, realistic steps to start feeling like myself again. Slowly, I noticed mornings felt lighter, I could focus better, and I genuinely started enjoying small things in life again. If you’ve been stuck in the cycle of burnout, don’t wait for some external fix. Give yourself the chance to reset. Reset Your Body and Mind by Morgan Lane was the guide that helped me get there, and it honestly felt like a lifeline when I needed it most.

I own an EAP company and this is not exactly right... burnout literally is having your central nervous system cooked from the intensity of your workload. Emails coming down range constantly, phone's going nuts, going outside the scope of your current position, loved ones relying on you, you become hyper-vigilant, feeling trapped, an intense level of anxiety and hyper-focus kicks in, fatigue turns to presenteeism, cortisol plagues the body, headaches, and morning depression. You become disconnect from life and people. It's literally like trying to break away from a warzone lol! Cooked

Naalala ko na naman sarili ko, ‘yung nagkagusto ako sa isang tao for 9 years (Hello sa‘yo, Neil! Haha!). Parang si Carson, pero ako naman ‘yung napaaga sa pag-amin. Maaga pa lang, nalaman na niya na may gusto ako sa kanya, tapos bigla na lang siyang lumayo. Siguro hindi niya alam paano ako iha-handle, o baka awkward lang talaga. Nung junior high, naghiwalay na rin kami kasi magkaibang school ang pinasukan namin. Akala ko tapos na ‘yung kwento namin doon. Pero dumating ang senior high, ako STEM student, siya ICT. Hindi ko alam na nasa parehong school pala kami. Nalaman ko lang nung nag-dropout ako at napagtripan ako ng kaibigan naming pareho na ichat siya. Ayun, nagkausap kami ulit, haha. That time, may girlfriend na siya. Pero kahit sandali lang ‘yung usapan namin, naging malinaw lahat. Sinabi niya kung bakit siya lumayo noon, at nakapagsorry rin siya. Doon ko na-realize na hindi lahat ng taong gusto natin ay mananatili, at hindi rin lahat ng feelings ay kailangang may kapalit. Tinanggap ko na lang. Hindi naging kami, pero naging okay ako. At least ngayon, nakagraduate na ako — buo, at wala nang bitbit na "what if." 😄🤍

Mình đọc rất nhiều cuốn tiểu thuyết. Nhưng mình thật sự bị nghiện bởi duy nhất cuốn sách này. Mình đã đọc đi đọc lại 4 lần nhưng vẫn kh thấy chán❤

1. Trì hoãn việc ôn luyện để thi. 2. Trì hoãn việc học tiếng Anh. 3. Luôn nói không có thời gian học nhưng khi có thì lại ngồi xem điện thoại. 4. Trì hoãn việc tập thể dục. 5. Không có kỷ luật bền vững. 6. Luôn lập kế hoạch rất chuyên nghiệp nhưng chỉ được 1-2 ngày lại đâu vào đấy. 7. Luôn có mục tiêu nhưng cứ nghĩ mình còn nhiều thời gian. 8. Luôn lên mạng tìm video động lực nhưng khi ngồi học thì lại mệt mỏi. 9. Mua sách về nhưng không bao giờ đọc. 10. Luôn suy nghĩ rất nhiều về cuộc sống trong lúc làm việc nhưng khi rảnh thì lại chơi game.

I really appreciate this video, even though it was a little alarming how every single step got worse and I was like, “oh yeah, been there…there too…that’s just a regular Tuesday.” I also really struggle with burnout because I have ADHD and I almost feel like my threshold for burnout is lower than the average person. I never want to be the weak link, whether it’s at work, in my family, friend group, etc. But I always overestimate what I’m able to get done and then I feel bad for getting tired or overwhelmed so quickly. I’m sure it’s part of this whole burnout cycle, but I haven’t figured out yet how to get out of it and like still…afford food and shelter and stuff. 🙃

It's like you're explaining my own thoughts to my in a way I haven't been able to. Thank you! <3

I've been feeling burnt out for the past 6 months from work. The stress keeps piling on until one day, I went into a mental breakdown. My body triggered some safety mechanism and all my stress melted away, however, at the same time I found it super hard to think straight. That's when I realized if I didn't stop it's going to lead to a long time to recover. If you're in that state or nearing the state because of constant stress, I recommend taking proper breaks or rest period. No job or school is worth destroying your mental or physical well being. Everything seems so large at that time but later on you realized its not so big at all. I'm quitting my job and going backpacking for awhile. I have enough funds to last me well over a couple months so I'll be taking some time to recuperate. If you don't have that opportunity, I recommend following the video. Yet, if its a job, try finding another one. It's not worth destroying your health and well-being over a job. There's plenty out there. If you're in school, follow what the video talks about. Also, maybe think about doing a backpacking trip. You can go on a low budget trip and still experience the world. Don't shirk your responsibility yet make sure it's not the pressure from society, friends, parents or family forcing you into staying at a position you don't want to be. Burnout is a sign that our body tells us there's something wrong. Something's not right. It's good because then we notice it to start changing our circumstances, routines and lifestyle. You are important and are full of dignity and self-worth. Jesus loves you. You are so important to Him you are worth dying for so that you can know God and be forgiven. Do not give up on yourself. God did not give up on you. You are of great value and worth to Him. Don't give up. If you can't do it, trust in Him. He will give you the strength to keep going each and every day. You are never alone. He is faithful always :) Jesus loves you!

This was really good, thanks. Modern life can be a real trap sometimes, particularly with career disillusionment. You pick a career (engineering in my case) and after 20 - 30 years you grow to despise it. Yet, you’re hitting middle-age, making good money and it’s nearly impossible to make a switch and maintain your obligations. Yes, you can pivot and “follow you passion” but leave a wake of disaster behind you for those depending on you. Life’s got you!

I finally stopped "testing" my partner to see if they’d leave. 💍 I was subconsciously trying to ruin a good thing because I didn't think I deserved it. I’d pick fights or be distant just to see if they’d still stay. The Hidden Trap of Self Sabotage by D. King helped me see how toxic that was. I stopped the games and started being honest. Our relationship is 100% better now that I’m not trying to blow it up every week. If you’re waiting for the "other shoe to drop," you’re probably the one holding the shoe.

I think this advice speaks to a very spesific group of people who are self employed or in a more flexible position. For me, this way of thinking falls down at the first step because the most work that needs to be done does not always create value. And even then, the value-generating work needed to be feasible is escalateing to the point of impossibly. My partner and I are coming to terms with the fact that after half a decade of grinding, hard work and forgoing many luxuries, we are effectively further away from even our most conservative goals. And it's because life involves family responsibility work, and medical emergency work, and social work, and trauma work, and unforseen accident work, and global pendemic work, and child care work. You could argue that there is value there, but it is not transferable. Hell, I would kill to have time to do something creative, or to take time to learn things that could put me in a better position or to financially benefit from the things I'm passionate about. But that means I'm risking rent or I'm coming up short for my meds or I'm sacrificing meals.

I graduated high school earlier this year, back in May. I got so burnt out, and it built up so much in the last 6 years of school, that I went through a full mental breakdown. It made it so I couldn't do even things that I loved. I tired out easily playing video games, couldn't read fanfiction (or any manner of written work, for that matter, with specific exceptions), couldn't listen to music (YouTube locking effective music playing behind paywalls didn't help), couldn't do any chores, had a mental block that made it impossible to so much as process the homework I'm looking at if I even manage to open it… you name it I lost it. It reduced me to watching YouTube and using Discord and browsing… certain sites. This was ONLY possible because I graduated, and my parents are letting me have a year off from doing anything and having a year before I get a job, because my parents are letting me rest for so long. I'm still recovering. I've started to regain the ability to do some chores, I have reignited my joy for listening to music, I have started going shopping or to restaurants with my family again, I can read things like news articles, I'm even able to play video games for longer and longer sessions. Still can't read fanfiction, still don't listen to music for long sessions, can't read books, still tire out really easily and quickly doing chores… still recovering, 7 months later. Currently I'm building a computer, and once I have that computer finished, and have a computer all to myself that can do pretty well anything, a lot of stuff is going to open up to me. I want to explore hobbies like art, music making, game development, coding, YouTube content creation, I want to FINALLY be able to play pc games, I want to get emulators so I can play retro games without paying hundreds of dollars to collectors for them… so many things I want to do. I think easing into some hobbies can help me with my burnout some. I hope I can finish recovering before my parents finally push me into a job.

At the basic end, it’s like relationships where you need to set boundaries. But… at the other end, including items you mention, it actually gets complex - because hours isn’t an easy measure like widgets. Why? Well, I’ve noticed that not only do I have a lot to learn at work, constantly, but so is everyone else - my bosses included. As such, what you describe is very advanced (yet straightforward). I’ve always been my hardest critic. But in early career it’s easy to see you dont have as much knowledge, experience etc., and I felt duty bound to catch up. But I’ve worked in an office environment where (for a few years during management change) demands were not made by bosses - you were expected to deliver. You could manage your time, say no, much like you’ve described. The problem herein was that some folk took liberty with it and slacked off. Now maybe they weren’t slacking, maybe that was their comfortable boundary… so how do you differentiate effort between staff; recognise the more productive person. It takes senior management to be completely in tune with all that, observe, and then promote/ pay rise those that deliver more - assuming pay/reward goes hand in hand with productivity/results. But that never worked. People putting in less effort still got promoted. Management changed too much; they were naive, not looking, too concerned with their own ladder gains etc. There is a lot of dynamics within many office environments, where what you’re suggesting can either : go unnoticed and cause imbalance & disgruntlement; or gets noticed in the wrong context - your 10% easeoff could look like laziness to the wrong boss. Balance; who’s right or wrong. Staying true to yourself, and setting boundaries, in a fair yet honest world might mean that you part ways with your employer because they don’t have the same work ethic as you; standards may be higher or lower; and therefore it may be a defining moment where you have to go and find work elsewhere. Finding your fit at work becomes harder. Many people don’t swap around jobs as much as that, trying to find the perfect fit, and instead will knuckle down and make do. When they are not in a happy place, some people will be disincentiveised and instead work less; and in contrast other people may knuckle down and work harder in the blind hope that they will keep their job, even get a pay rise, or at least remain duty bound within their own moral core. I’ve always said that those around me have nothing to do with me in terms of my own performance. And yet, I have been in that position, where I drove myself to a standard that probably went unrecognised for a long time; my standard simply being that I deliver of what’s being asked, and I always think you should deliver a full and complete job without errors. But seeing those around me taking an easier time of it and still getting promoted starts to get under the skin. After two burnouts, which I felt were only slightly unreasonable on my health, but very quickly redeemed by having a couple of weeks off, I thought I could keep running at that pace… But it was the third burnout where all the wheels fell off the wagon. And things get much harder. I’ve always said to myself that I wanted a better life work balance, and this time I think it’s paramount that I do make that change. It means that I will have to set those boundaries myself,… For example, I have interviewed for jobs mentioning a need for a part-time basis; I would like one day off a week where I can alternate between doing some voluntary work, and some hobby crafts; but every employer I talk to about part-time immediately switches off and says that that is a serious no go. Employers do not like the sound of people easing back a bit. I find their attitude utterly ridiculous considering how incredibly hard I have worked - but they don’t know me from Adam. There are people that claim they would do 60 hours per week, but when you look at it they manage to fit in many tea breaks, walking around, doing some easy stuff. That is nothing like working full on for 50 to 60 hours Per week, with no breaks, no distractions to attention, and focusing the entire time. It is mentally exhausting. Where’s the balance; what’s fair? Therefore, nothing changes because for many it doesn’t look broke. Set your own boundaries & targets… but understand consequences. Others will often say “play the game/system”.

I find being in nature is very relaxing and refreshing! Also personally having no expectations for a day makes a big difference to how stressed I feel. I like to paint, just simple plants, but it's very helpful to remind myself to calm down and slowly paint each leaf at a time, that it's ok to not be in a rush- and it always makes the final product turn out better than if I had rushed it! It's nice having that reward, as I have ADHD. I apply this also in daily life, like washing my hair very slowly, or doing slow stretches, 10/10 recommend. And don't underestimate how much just sitting in the sun without any other stimulation can help you either! Remember to breath slowly and take your time ☺️

Working at fast food does not give you that option. Mainly because its a job were you're suppose to deliver a service or role and be able to give it consistently and fast. Some jobs like doing the dishes in the back is easy; no real rush and you can listen to music and podcasts because no one will see you either than the manager and your co-workers and it doesn't require you to listen to someone else, so what I like to do is just before work like the night of my day off, I would take all the music I like and put it in a collection list and have it on autoplay, then when I goto work and I get put back on dishes; I can simply just put on my headphones, my cleaning gear like gloves and apron and clean away while listening to my favorite music. Because of the simplicity of doing dishes: I can listen to music without impairing my ability to do the dishes. However if you're a cashier, fry cook, or responsible for making sure the order a customer ordered is right and delivering it to them, you can't just listen to music. You can't hear orders, Customers will complain and then the manager will have to inform you that you can't wear headphones at work or play music, even if you're the only one playing it out loud, because nobody cares about your taste in music. With the flexible scheduling where the employee has control over their own schedule, you can either end up being so overstaffed that the managers just send some people home or understaffed where they have to either close half the store like only allowing drive thru orders to be taken or close entirely and in instances where the drive-thru is the only thing that's open; now we have even more complaints over their order taking so long to be made. I don't have much faith in my workplace, Letalone for fast-food employment anymore since work is already draining in of itself and making food really fast seems not only like the most unproductive compared to what I could be doing, but also like an impossibility, considering the fact that the food I usually eat takes more than 15 minutes to make and i find that more enjoyable than whatever I do at work. we're understaffed, and that no one gets paid sick leave so they end up going to work and hiding their illness and bring down an illness onto nearly everyone that works there, and to top it all off: a person in line that is going hangry because he didn't get his order the moment he parked at the window where he would get his meal .

In all honesty and no offense meant, this is really just scratching the surface about causes for burnouts. The true cause lies in all the negative/limiting beliefs that live in your unconscious mind, which have been build up during your lifetime (often originating in early childhood). As long as you don't transform these beliefs (via hypnosis), burnout is just around any corner. Independent of work culture, or other external factors. The latter are NEVER the root cause of burnout, it's the stories you tell yourself. Not allowing yourself to respect your boundaries, desperately trying to meet other people's expectations in order to get 'approval' (i'm good enough), etc etc. As long as you seek self-approval outside yourself and not truly transform your beliefs, you'll never be free. I've been in a very heavy burnout and dealt with a shitload of limiting beliefs. 5 years later, I'm a completely different person, feeling truly free to live my life according to my own desires. Thanks to hypnosis, done by someone who actually knows what she's doing. But as hypnosis is not in the interest of the 'healthcare industry', it will always be set aside as a fairytale. So unfair, but that's what the collective conscious has been learned to belief. I could talk for hours on this subject. Feel free to reach out. Kind regards, Paul

Khi mình còn học lớp 6, mình đã có một người bạn. Người bạn ấy có thể là niềm vui khi đến trường của mình nhưng rồi lại biến thành nỗi sợ cho mình khi đến trường. Đó là sự cô đơn. Ban đầu chỉ có 2 người chúng mình chơi với nhau vì hai đứa cũng là bạn cùng bạn, nhưng cho tới cuối học kỳ lớp 6 cậu ấy bắt đầu có những người bạn mới. Lúc đầu mình thấy vậy là vẫn ổn. Nhưng qua nhiều ngày, mình bắt đầu BIẾN MẤT giữa tình bạn ấy. Ngày qua ngày, tình bạn giữa chúng ta ngày càng xa cách, và rồi tôi không đi chơi với đám bạn của cậu ấy nữa, vì khi đi với họ cảm giác lạc lõng, cô đơn cứ luôn xâm chiếm đầu tôi. Cứ như vậy, tôi tạo cho chính mình một bức tường ngăn cách mình với cả thế giới, thậm chí là gia đình. Có vài lần mình đã muốn đập vỡ cái bức tường ấy, cố gắng kết bạn với những người bạn mới, nhưng vẫn là cảm giác lac lõng ấy. Và thế mình CÔ ĐƠN cho tới lớp 8. Mình là một đứa hướng nội vì thế nên cũng khá khó khi nói chuyện và kết bạn với mọi người. Nhưng rồi xem video này của Anh Trí thì đã có thể nhìn lại và suy nghĩ về qua khứ của mình. Autistic burnout is 110% real and it sucks, bad. I didn't know it at the time, but I went through severe autistic burnout that, arguably I'm still recovering from 7 years later. I considered the possibility of being autistic around the time I had started high school, around 2014. Everyone put me down and told me that I'm making it up and I just need to suck it up. I sucked it up. Until I literally could not deal with it anymore. I started skipping school, and eventually dropping out. My mental health completely crumbled and I essentially went into hiding from the world and society. I tried asking my family for help in getting therapy or SOMETHING but again I was denied for years until 2021. My therapist has helped me immensely and I eventually got my autism diagnosis at the age of 22, a little over 2 years ago now. I'm still struggling, but at least I understand myself better and I have access to more resources and support I haven't for most of my life.

Seems to me that burnout can be described as or even equated to mental exhaustion, mostly caused by an unsustainable daily routine. People are very quick to notice, recognize and treat physical exhaustion by taking breaks from whatever, whenever needed. Mental exhaustion is largely unnoticed and ignored until it becomes impossible to go on without treating it, or until it causes even more mental problems, like depression. I think that as long as the society in general keeps treating the physical aspects of the human body much more seriously than the mental, there's no chance whatsoever to live in such a society without experiencing "burnout" sooner or later, with very few & lucky exceptions. Most people's daily adult lives do not meet the minimum requirements for a healthy mind. Far from it.

This song always reminded me of someone whom I've been estranged with for several years now. We were never really together, in fact, we were just friends back in college. We always had this unspoken/not unspoken affection towards each other, but we never really acted out on it or did something about it. We've both acknowleged it to each other, so we kinda always have known that about us. It wasn't really a secret, but we don't talk about it that much. Our closest friends have always known also. A lot has happened already and so much time has passed. We both had our own share of relationships in the past, and currently in our respective relationships as well. And yet, there's always something gravitating us both towards each other. The connection was there, it never really went away even if we don't talk for months. It's so weird because I know how I've always felt about him, and vice versa, and yet I'm also always unsure of a future with him. But, I never wanted to lose him, he'll always have a place in my life, even if that means we'll never be together. I don't want to lose my connection with him, but I wanna set him free of any remote possibility or chance that we'll be together, because we won't ever be.

I’ve just lost my reason to try hard in school. I did my absolute best in middle school (sometimes on accident) but I switched to homeschool for my high school years. I’m a sophomore this year but the time flew by so fast I still feel like a freshman. I’ve really only seen my old friends a handful of times since middle school graduation. It’s like I’m still stuck in the past. I try reaching out and we keep planning to get together sometime but it never goes anywhere. They’re good people but it just feels like I’m not a priority. Not that I really should be, they all have new friends and are probably just moving on. It’s been 2 years anyway. I haven’t made any new strong friendships in that time. I have no one to compare myself to and I’m just home alone so much of the time. Family time doesn’t fill the hole of good friends. I’m not invited to things and I don’t even know if I’d show up if I was. I never have plans so I just procrastinate school all day. I play video games (usually alone) during practically all my free time but at the same time it doesn’t get me anywhere. I’m not really getting better at any of the games. My school days are usually 8-4 or 6 depending on my motivation and yet I still don’t get all my work done. Of course I get an A anyway even though I’m incredibly behind. I don’t always feel I deserve it, I probably don’t. I used to go above and beyond, and sometimes I still do, but at the cost of my only free time. I’m about to stop inviting my old friends who are somehow never available and just accept it. That’s pretty much how I’ve been living anyway. I’m in a mood right now, and I can’t tell if this is tired me or real me. Maybe it’s both. I really should be rambling this into my journal that I’ve been neglecting but whatever. Dunno if anyone will ever read this but whatever. I honestly don’t know if any of that made sense or how I got all this from a vid about burnout. I have no idea what this is. Most of these emotions I can’t even identify so how do I find and fix the root? Wow long yap session. I need to sleep, I have class in 6 hours

I'm not sure if I'm still in burnout or past it. Now, it's just the inability to feel hope or joy. I've been trying for years to get help, but due to systemic issues and medical gaslighting, I don't know if that's possible anymore. I have CPTSD, and I am 30, but I still live with my abusive parent, and I've been isolated my entire life from every single thing. Isolated from my dad, isolated from society, and even isolated from school after the moving started happening every half a year. I don't know what's positive and what's not. I also feel trapped, like I can't get out of this, so now I just deal with it. I have been living with the thought, "I won't die because I don't want to be someone else's trauma," but I've been going months on that reason or excuse to keep going and having no joy or hope other than that—nothing. It's constant cognitive dissonance. It hurts a lot, living for everything but myself. It's painful in ways I can't explain. My fiancé, who lives in America, is trying to help me get to him so he can get me help, both physical and mental, because I suffer from fluctuating brain dysfunction caused by severe health conditions that the doctors still haven't investigated, and it's been 6 years. I've finally started getting taken seriously, or at least they now have proof I'm unwell and delirious, but there's no help yet other than throwing me on 2 weeks of Lorazepam with no refills. So, I've been barely taking it out of fear of running out and then nothing changing. If I feel happy temporarily only to feel miserable right after, then it would hurt even more.

Perfect break: Turn off phone. Go into the forest, meditate a bit, enjoy the fresh air and nature, listen to a bit of music, take a nice bath, go to bed early. To me, you know, yes breaks are an important part of burn out recovery, but I wouldn't overestimate the (long term) impact, because what made you not care for yourself in the first place? What makes you feel like you are not validated in ypur relationships or at work? Becaue your partner and colleagues are arseholes or maybe because you were abused as a child and neglected and told yourself you weren't worthy and nobody liked you because you were bad in order to survive? Or what sucks your energy really? Is it that your work consumes so much energy or that maybe you have some trauma that holds your life energy back. And you know, what I consider really important is this: Don't expect different outcomes if you put in the same things over and over again. So even if you have taken a break, I'm sorry but it's not gonna be enough, you are going to find yourself struggling again if you don't go to the roots of the problems and make a bigger change. (But at first taking a break is also important.)

The pattern I see in every burned out person who comes through my workshops is the same: their nervous system has forgotten how to switch between activation and recovery. They are stuck on the gas pedal. The breath is the fastest way to change that because it is the only autonomic function you can also control voluntarily. Five minutes of coherent breathing at 6 breaths per minute, done consistently every morning, can measurably shift your heart rate variability within two weeks.

I just realized today that I have the symptoms of burnout syndrome (and it's sad to think that I just realized it when I'm already near the last phase). I'm still a student, and I work at a campus publication. The thing is, I am not paid to do graphic works and articles. At first, I was enjoying it. But since our Editor-in-chief was replaced by a fellow coworker, the whole mood of the office has also changed. We went from a carefree editor to a very strict you-can't-impress-me editor. Doing articles and graphics became mechanical for me, as if it was judged based on how applicable it would be, rather than based on its content. I actually have no problems with our editor. It's just that doing all the publication-related stuff was becoming a form of job-completion instead of an achievement. And the reason why I can't get out of it is because without me, the publication would suffer. (Many people had already quit. There are less than 10 workers writing for an audience of 5,000 students). Not to mention, I would be called selfish if I left the organization in its ailing state. So yeah. I'm stuck, but I'm hoping for a recovery after I actually see a psychologist.

- Not caring what others think has its pros and cons. - Everyone has their own life. Choose the way you want to live, peacefully, and sleep well. - Everything is a choice. Finding happiness from the outside is just a quick stimulation and doesn't fill the void inside. - What did you do well and enjoy as a child that everyone praised? A basic minimalist lifestyle, being yourself. That's what helps you relieve stress. - People always need each other. Sellers need buyers. Everyone in life has a talent that helps their life. - Self-esteem is a form of arrogance, thinking you know everything, not listening to the truth, liking pretense. - There is no one in this life to trust more than yourself. Belief is the power that helps you increase your strength. Trust yourself first. - Accepting that the speed of growth of two people is not suitable, you must choose to step out or blame. Learn to heal, forgive yourself and that person at that time so as not to carry that pain throughout your life, not to let that negative energy dominate your life. - Be grateful for the smallest things, even if they are the most normal to the most trivial. For example, the parts of your body.

The one good thing that came from the Pandemic is it taught me how unsustainable the life I was living pre-covid was. I was working 10 hours a day minimum at a high stress job that made me feel important, and paid better than any job I had before, and kept me from experiencing depression and for that reason I thought I was doing well. I describe working at this job as working with a gun to my head all the time, and even if I worked literally 24/7 it still wouldn’t have been enough to please everyone who needed stuff from me. Then 2020 hit and I learned that I was not needed, I was being used. And the second the lockdown happened I was out the door. Now I prioritize things that are mine. My art, my stories, my family, my friends. I am technically working just as much and as long as before now, but since it’s on the right things and it’s within my control, I am able to take appropriate breaks and I am avoiding burn out (so far at least).
